items.passage: When a product is scarce, its perceived value tends to rise. People interpret limited availability as a sign that the item is desirable, so they feel a stronger urge to obtain it before it is gone. In this way, scarcity can ( A ) demand even when the product's actual quality stays the same.
items.stem: 빈칸 (A)에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 희소성이 (품질이 그대로여도) 수요를 끌어올린다(boost)는 흐름.

items.passage: Under the gold standard, a country's money was tied to a fixed amount of gold. This system limited how much currency a government could print, which helped keep prices stable. However, it also meant that the economy could not easily ( A ) to sudden shocks, since the money supply was bound to gold reserves.
items.stem: 빈칸 (A)에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 통화량이 금에 묶여 충격에 '적응(adapt)'하기 어려웠다는 대조(however).

items.passage: Astronaut Yujin, ①floating in zero gravity, ②recorded her observations, ③surrounding by the silence of space, while the Earth ④appeared far ⑤below.
items.stem: 밑줄 친 부분 중 어법상 틀린 것은?
items.explain: ③ surrounding → surrounded (우주의 침묵에 '둘러싸인' 수동 → 과거분사).

items.lead: Not only ( A ) the new system reduce costs, but it also improved safety.

items.explain: 부정어(Not only) 문두 도치 + 과거시제 → did the system reduce.

items.passage:
Bears prepare for winter in a remarkable way.
(A) During this dormancy, their heart rate and body temperature drop dramatically.
(B) They eat large amounts of food to store energy as body fat.
(C) Then, as the cold arrives, they enter a deep sleep called hibernation.
items.stem: 주어진 글 다음에 이어질 순서로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 먹어서 지방 저장(B) → 추위에 동면 진입(C) → 그 동면 동안 심박·체온 하락(A).

items.lead: 〈보기〉 However, this freedom came at a cost.
items.passage: The internet allowed anyone to publish information. ( ① ) Suddenly, ordinary people could share their ideas with the world. ( ② ) Anyone could also spread false or harmful content. ( ③ ) As a result, societies had to find new ways to judge what was true. ( ④ ) Today, that challenge is bigger than ever. ( ⑤ )
items.stem: 글의 흐름상 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳은?
items.explain: '자유에는 대가가 따랐다' 뒤에 거짓·유해 콘텐츠 확산이 이어져야 자연스러움 → ②.

items.passage: Pop music's accessibility makes it ①popular across cultures. Its simple melodies are easy to ②remember, and streaming services have made songs instantly ③available. This wide ④reach has ⑤decreased its global influence.

items.explain: ⑤ decreased → increased (넓은 도달 범위는 영향력을 '키운다').

items.lead: The sunk cost fallacy makes people (A) a project simply because they have already invested in it, even when stopping would be the (B) choice.
items.stem: (A), (B)에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 매몰비용 오류는 이미 투자했다는 이유로 '계속(continue)'하게 만든다(멈추는 게 '더 현명(wiser)').

items.passage: According to the theory of relativity, massive objects bend the space around them. Light traveling near such objects follows this curved space, so its path bends too. This effect, called gravitational lensing, lets astronomers observe stars that are actually hidden behind massive galaxies. The bent light can even appear as multiple images of a single star.
items.stem: 윗글의 내용과 일치하는 것은?
options: Light always travels in a perfectly straight line.
Massive objects have no effect on nearby space.
Gravitational lensing hides stars from astronomers.
Bent light can show multiple images of one star.
Relativity claims space cannot be curved.
items.explain: 휘어진 빛이 한 별의 여러 상(image)으로 보일 수 있음.

items.passage: According to the theory of relativity, massive objects bend the space around them. Light traveling near such objects follows this curved space, so its path bends too. This effect, called gravitational lensing, lets astronomers observe stars that are actually hidden behind massive galaxies. The bent light can even appear as multiple images of a single star.
items.stem: 윗글의 내용과 일치하지 않는 것은?
options: Massive objects bend the space around them.
Light near massive objects follows curved space.
A single star may appear as several images.
Light is never affected by gravity.
items.explain: 빛은 중력의 영향을 받으므로 '전혀 받지 않는다'는 불일치.

items.lead: 우리가 불리한 결과를 얻을 때, 어떤 면에서 우리가 '가장 듣고 싶지 않은' 말은 그 과정이 공정했다는 말이다. 불리한 결과와 불공정한 과정의 결합이 분노를 일으키지만, 이 결합은 또한 위로의 상, 즉 나쁜 결과를 우리 자신 이외의 다른 무언가의 탓으로 돌릴 가능성을 더불어 가져다준다.
items.stem: As ____ as the combination of an unfavorable outcome and an unfair process is, this combination also brings with it a consolation prize: the possibility of attributing the bad outcome to something other than ourselves.
items.explain: outraging — 우리가 불리한 결과를 얻을 때, 어떤 면에서 우리가 '가장 듣고 싶지 않은' 말은 그 과정이 공정했다는 말이

items.lead: 우리는 우리의 나쁜 결과가 우리와는 거의 관련이 없었고 불공정한 과정과 전적으로 관련이 있었다고 믿음으로써 우리 자신을 안심시킬지도 모른다.
items.stem: We may ____ ourselves by believing that our bad outcome had little to do with us and everything to do with the unfair process.
items.explain: reassure — 우리는 우리의 나쁜 결과가 우리와는 거의 관련이 없었고 불공정한 과정과 전적으로 관련이 있었다고 믿음으로써

items.passage: We collect stamps, coins, vintage cars even when they serve no practical purpose. The post office doesn't accept the old stamps, the banks don't take old coins, and the vintage cars are no longer allowed on the road. These are all side issues; the attraction is that they are in short supply. In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness – with the agreement that afterward they could keep one poster as a reward for their participation. Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. The poster that was no ionger available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance: when we are ____________ of an option, we suddenly deem it more attractive.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 정답 ③ · 출처: 13강 7번 · 2024년 6월 고2 학평 31번

items.passage: [24년 6월 32번] If we've invested in something that hasn't repaid us ― be it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship ― it is not ⓐeasy for us to leave. This is the sunk cost fallacy. Our instinct is to continue investing money or time as we anticipate that our investment will ⓑpay off in the end. Giving up would mean acknowledging that we've wasted something we can't get back, and that thought is so ⓒunpleasant that we prefer to avoid it if we can. The problem, of course, is that if something really is a bad bet, then staying with it simply increases the amount we ⓓgain. Rather than walk away from a bad five-year relationship, for example, we turn it into a bad 10-year relationship; rather than accept that we've lost a thousand dollars, we lay down another thousand and lose that too. In the end, by ⓔpostponing the agony of acknowledging our problem, we merely make it worse. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.

items.passage: [24년 9월 31번] When we get an unfavorable outcome, in some ways the last thing we want to hear is that the process was fair. As outraging as the combination of an unfavorable outcome and an unfair process is, this combination also brings with it a consolation prize: the possibility of attributing the bad outcome to something other than ourselves. We may reassure ourselves by believing that our bad outcome had little to do with us and everything to do with the unfair process. If the process is fair, however, we cannot nearly as easily externalize the outcome; we got what we got "fair and square." When the process is fair we believe that our outcome is deserved, which is another way of saying that ______________________________.
items.stem: 다음 글의 빈칸에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: the result came from something within ourselves
outcomes are usually beyond our personal control
the outcome could have changed in another process
luck occasionally determines whether we succeed or fail
an unfair process tends to make accepting the result feel easier

The post office doesn’t accept the old stamps, the banks don‘t take old coins, and the vintage cars are ’no longer allowed on the road. These are all side issues: the attraction is that they are in short supply. In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness – with the agreement that afterward they could keep one poster as a reward for their participation. Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. The poster that was no longer available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance: when we are deprived of an option, we suddenly deem it more attractive.

items.passage: [24년 6월 32번] If we've invested in something that hasn't repaid us ― be it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship ― we find it very difficult to walk away. This is the sunk cost fallacy. Our instinct is to continue investing money or time as we hope that our investment will prove to be worthwhile in the end. Giving up would mean acknowledging that we've wasted something we can't get back, and that thought is so painful that we prefer to avoid it if we can. The problem, of course, is that if something really is a bad bet, then staying with it simply increases the amount we lose. Rather than walk away from a bad five-year relationship, for example, we turn it into a bad 10-year relationship; rather than accept that we've lost a thousand dollars, we lay down another thousand and lose that too. In the end, by delaying the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.
items.stem: 다음 글의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: How to Get Back Our Losses from a Fraud
Move Forward: Don't Let Sink Costs Hold You Back
Stick to the Plan: The Best Way to Avoid Losses
Sunk Costs: A Necessary Condition for Investment

items.passage: [25년 6월 31번] We might forget an anecdote about a stranger because it makes few connections with our existing associations, but we won't forget a piece of gossip about our cousin. There's one complex network that is larger and quicker to access than all others─the self. We've been thinking about ourselves in our whole lives. (In fact, there were entire years during junior high when we weren't capable of thinking about much else.) So, __________________________. It hits even closer to home than our actual home─we can take a vacation away from our home, but not from ourselves. The most effective communicators find ways to make the abstract. Consider the warning that law schools give to motivate first year law students concerning the rigors of their program. Hearing that "the first year dropout rate is 33%" is an abstract statistic. "Look to your left, look to your right. One of the three of you won't be joining us next fall" wakes up the self.
items.stem: 윗글의 빈칸 (A)에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: specific examples are remembered more easily than general ideas
we remember gossip better than facts because it is more amusing
the brain stores more memories from adolescence than from adulthood
we remember self-related information more effectively than unrelated facts
emotional stories usually leave stronger impressions than logical ones

items.passage: [25년 6월 32번] Steve Jobs used analogy to get people to embrace the new technology. Before computers, people worked in a physical world. We used paper and pens and ⓐphysical file folders and so on. The idea of working in a virtual world was radically different. Or at least seemed radically different. What Jobs understood was that a physical office was fundamentally ⓑirrelevant to a virtual office. To win over the masses, Jobs drew strong analogies between the traditional workplace people knew well with the new, unfamiliar ⓒvirtual workplace. In the pre-computer workplace, when ideas were written on paper we called this a 'document'. When those documents needed to be stored they were put in a 'folder'. And those folders were kept on a 'desk'. Documents, folders, and desktops are the terms we use in our virtual work because Steve Jobs understood that using ⓓfamiliar terms would make the new technology easier to understand. The ⓔparallels between the physical and virtual workplace now seem obvious.

In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness – with the agreement that afterward they could keep one poster as a reward for their participation. Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. The poster that was no longer available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance: when we are deprived of an option, we suddenly deem it more attractive.

items.passage: [25년 6월 32번] Steve Jobs used analogy to get people to embrace the new technology. Before computers, people worked in a physical world. We used paper and pens and physical file folders and so on. The idea of working in a virtual world was radically different. Or at least seemed radically different. What Jobs understood was that a physical office was fundamentally similar to a virtual office. To win over the masses, Jobs drew strong analogies between the traditional workplace people knew well with the new, unfamiliar virtual workplace. In the pre-computer workplace, when ideas were written on paper it was called ... a document. When those documents needed to be stored they were put in ... a folder. And those folders were kept on ... a desk. Documents, folders, and desktops are the terms we use in our virtual work because Steve Jobs understood that using familiar terms would make the new technology easier to understand.

items.passage: [24년 6월 32번] If we've invested in something that hasn't repaid us - be it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship - we feel that it would all have been for nothing if we quit. For example, you decided to write a book, and five years later you're still writing it. The topic that once interested you no longer does, and it's a ⓐstruggle to get yourself to sit down at your desk and start typing. But you're two hundred pages in and have ⓑdedicated hundreds of hours to researching and writing your novel. At this point, you're considering ⓒabandoning the project. But something stops you - that nagging reminder that you already put so much time and effort into the work. If you don't finish it, you think you'll have wasted all of that energy. That nagging reminder has a name: the sunk cost fallacy. The sunk cost fallacy is a logical fallacy that entails ⓓsticking with a losing venture because you've already invested a significant amount of time, money, or other resources that you can't get back. It ⓔcontradicts the idea that because you've already incurred costs, you need to continue with this endeavor in order to "get your money's worth." If you're familiar with the phrase "cut your losses," that's a call to avoid getting trapped by the sunk cost fallacy.

items.passage: [24년 6월 32번] If we've invested in something that hasn't repaid us - be it money in a failing venture, or time in an ⓐunhappy relationship - we find it very difficult to walk away. This is the sunk cost fallacy. Our instinct is to continue investing money or time as we hope that our investment will prove to be ⓑworthwhile in the end. Giving up would mean ⓒacknowledging that we've wasted something we can't get back, and that thought is so painful that we prefer to avoid it if we can. The problem, of course, is that if something really is a bad bet, then staying with it simply ⓓincreases the amount we lose. Rather than walk away from a bad five-year relationship, for example, we turn it into a bad 10-year relationship; rather than accept that we've lost a thousand dollars, we lay down another thousand and lose that too. In the end, by ⓔhastening the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.

items.passage: [25년 6월 31번] We might forget an anecdote about a stranger because it makes few connections with our existing associations, but we won't forget a piece of gossip about our cousin. There's one complex network that is larger and quicker to access than all others - the self. We've been thinking about ourselves in our whole lives. (In fact, there were entire years during junior high when we weren't capable of thinking about much else.) So if a new piece of information has something to do with us, ________________________ _______. It hits even closer to home than our actual home - we can take a vacation away from our home, but not from ourselves. The most effective communicators find ways to make the abstract personal. Consider the warning that law schools give to motivate first-year law students concerning the rigors of their program. Hearing that "the first-year dropout rate is 33%" is an abstract statistic. "Look to your left, look to your right. One of the three of you won't be joining us next fall" wakes up the self. *rigor: 엄격함

items.passage: [24년 6월 31번] We collect stamps, coins, vintage cars even when they serve no practical purpose. The post office doesn’t accept the old stamps, the banks don’t take old coins, and the vintage cars are no longer allowed on the road. These are all side issues; the attraction is that they are in short supply. In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness – with the agreement that afterward they could keep one poster as a reward for their participation. Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. The poster that was no longer available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance: when we are deprived of an option, we suddenly deem it more attractive.

Urgency of Collecting: Why We Accumulate
An Effective Way to Make Choices When Scarcity Strikes

items.passage: [25년 6월 32번] Steve Jobs used analogy to get people to embrace the new technology. (A) These workspace elements are the terms we use in our virtual work because Steve Jobs understood that using familiar terms would make the new technology easier to understand. The parallels between the physical and virtual workplace now seem obvious. (B) To win over the masses, Jobs drew strong analogies between the traditional workplace people knew well with the new, unfamiliar virtual workplace. In the pre computer workplace, when ideas were written on paper it was called a document. When those documents needed to be stored they were put in a folder. And those folders were kept on a desk. (C) Before computers, people worked in a physical world. We used paper and pens and physical file folders and so on. The idea of working in a virtual world was radically different. Or at least seemed radically different. What Jobs understood was that a physical office was fundamentally similar to a virtual office.

items.passage: [24년 9월 31번] When we get an unfavorable outcome, in some ways the last thing we want to hear is that the process was fair. As outraging as the combination of an unfavorable outcome and an unfair process is, this combination also brings with it a consolation prize: the possibility of attributing the bad outcome to something other than ourselves. We may reassure ourselves by believing that our bad outcome had little to do with us and everything to do with the unfair process. If the process is fair, however, we cannot nearly as easily externalize the outcome; we got what we got "fair and square." When the process is fair we believe that our outcome is deserved, which is another way of saying that there must have been something about ourselves (what we did or who we are) that caused the outcome.

options: Don't Judge a Book by Its Cover
Unfair Processes: Easy to Shift the Blame
A Double-Edged Sword: Facing Their Own Demerits
Look Before You Leap: Insights on Risk from Process

(In fact, there were entire years during junior high when we weren't capable of thinking about much else.) So if a new piece of information has something to do with us, it will be more easily and thoroughly processed. It hits even closer to home than our actual home ─ we can take a vacation away from our home, but not from ourselves. The most effective communicators find ways to make the abstract personal. items.passage: [24년 9월 31번] When we get an unfavorable outcome, in some ways the last thing we want to hear is that the process was fair. As outraging as the combination of an unfavorable outcome and an unfair process is, this combination also brings with it a consolation prize: the possibility of attributing the bad outcome to something other than ourselves. We may reassure ourselves by believing that our bad outcome had little to do with us and everything to do with the unfair process. If the process is fair, however, we cannot nearly as easily externalize the outcome; we got what we got "fair and square." When the process is fair we believe that our outcome is deserved, which is another way of saying that there must have been something about ourselves (what we did or who we are) that caused the outcome. *consolation: 위로

options: unknown risks to fair process from unfavorable outcome
efforts to attribute one's performance to employer's prejudice
positive effects of externalizing what we got on self-perception
attribution of undesirable outcomes depending on process fairness

items.passage: [24년 6월 32번] If we’ve invested in something that hasn’t repaid us – be it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship – we find ⓐit very difficult to walk away. This is the sunk cost fallacy. Our instinct is to continue ⓑinvesting money on time as we hope that our investment will prove to be worthwhile in the end. Giving up would mean acknowledging that we’ve wasted something we can’t get back, and that thought is so ⓒpainful that we prefer to avoid it if we can. The problem, of course, is that if something really is a bad bet, then staying with it simply ⓓincrease the amount we lose. Rather than walk away from a bad five-year relationship, for example, we turn it into a bad 10-year relationship; rather than accept that we’ve lost a thousand dollars, we lay down another thousand and lose ⓔthat too. In the end, by postponing the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.

items.passage: [25년 6월 31번] We might forget an anecdote about a stranger because it makes few connections with our existing associations, but we won't forget a piece of gossip about our cousin. There's one complex network that is larger and quicker to access than all others ─ the self. We've been thinking about ourselves in our whole lives. (In fact, there were entire years during junior high when we weren't capable of thinking about much else.) So if a new piece of information has something to do with us, it will be more easily and thoroughly processed. It hits even closer to home than our actual home ─ we can take a vacation away from our home, but not from ourselves. The most effective communicators find ways to make the abstract personal. Consider the warning that law schools give to motivate first-year law students concerning the rigors of their program. Hearing that "the first-year dropout rate is 33%" is an abstract statistic. "Look to your left, look to your right. One of the three of you won't be joining us next fall" wakes up the self.
items.stem: 다음 글의 요지로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Humans have limitations in remembering new information
We tend to consider information about others more important
Information related to oneself is processed more effectively
Objective information is considered more significant than personal information
Abstract statistical data is useful for understanding oneself

items.passage: [24년 6월 31번] We collect stamps, coins, vintage cars even when they serve no practical purpose. The post office doesn’t accept the old stamps, the banks don’t take old coins, and the vintage cars are no longer allowed on the road. These are all side issues; the attraction is that they are in short supply. In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness – with the agreement that afterward they could keep one poster as a reward for their participation. Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. The poster that was no longer available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance: when we are deprived of an option, we suddenly deem it more attractive.

The idea of working in a virtual world was radically different. Or at least seemed radically different. What Jobs understood was that a physical office was fundamentally similar to a virtual office. And those folders were kept on … a desk. Documents, folders, and desktops are the terms we use in our virtual work because Steve Jobs understood that using familiar terms would make the new technology easier to understand. The parallels between the physical and virtual workplace now seem obvious.

items.passage: [24년 9월 31번] When we get an unfavorable outcome, in some ways the last thing we want to hear is that the process was fair. As outraging as the combination of an unfavorable outcome and an unfair process is, this combination also brings with it a consolation prize: the possibility of attributing the bad outcome to something other than ourselves. We may reassure ourselves by believing that our bad outcome had little to do with us and everything to do with the unfair process. If the process is fair, however, we cannot nearly as easily externalize the outcome; we got what we got "fair and square." When the process is fair we believe that our outcome is deserved, which is another way of saying that there must have been something about ourselves (what we did or who we are) that caused the outcome.
items.stem: 다음 글의 주제로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: problems of social inequality caused by unjust processes
why objective assessment is challenging in subjective contexts
strategies for self-reassurance in the face of disappointing results
psychological impact of process fairness on accepting unfavorable outcomes
issues related to externalizing an unfavorable outcome in the workplace
items.explain: 정답 ④ · 출처: 13강 6번 · 2024년 9월 고2 학평 31번

items.passage: [24년 6월 31번] We collect stamps, coins, vintage cars even when they serve no practical purpose. The post office doesn’t accept the old stamps, the banks don’t take old coins, and the vintage cars are no longer allowed on the road. These are all side issues; the attraction is that they are in short supply. In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness – with the agreement that afterward they could keep one poster as a reward for their participation. Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. The poster that was no longer available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance: when we are deprived of an option, we suddenly deem it more attractive.
items.stem: 다음 글의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Vintage Cars: Unique Design Makes Craving
Reactance: Every Action Has an Equal and Opposite Reaction
Keep Every Poster for Participation Experience
items.explain: 정답 ⑤ · 출처: 13강 7번 · 2024년 6월 고2 학평 31번

items.passage: [25년 6월 31번] Our brains are complex webs of associations, and we can access a piece of information in many different ways. The more a new fact connects with the existing network of associations in our brains, the more likely we are to remember it. We might forget an anecdote about a stranger because it makes few connections with our existing associations, but we won't forget a piece of gossip about our cousin. There's one complex network that is larger and quicker to access than all others – the self. We've been thinking about ourselves our whole lives. (In fact, there were entire years during junior high when we weren't capable of thinking about much else.) So if a new piece of information has something to do with us, it will be more easily and thoroughly processed. It hits even closer to home than our actual home – we can take a vacation away from our home, but not from ourselves. The most effective communicators find ways to make the abstract personal. Consider the warning that law schools give to motivate first-year law students concerning the rigors of their program. Hearing that "the first-year dropout rate is 33%" is an abstract statistic. "Look to your left, look to your right. One of the three of you won't be joining us next fall" wakes up the self. We feel something.
items.stem: 다음 글의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Make It Personal: "This Is About You"
Speak to the General You, and That'll Be Heard
The Magic of Turning Statistics into the Abstract
Avoid Numbers: Perfect Translations Don't Need Numbers
items.explain: 정답 ② · 출처: 13강 1번 · 2025년 6월 고2 학평 31번

items.passage: [24년 6월 32번] If we've invested in something that hasn't repaid us ― ⓐbe it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship ― we find it very difficult to walk away. This is the sunk cost fallacy. Our instinct is to continue ⓑinvesting money or time as we hope that our investment will prove to be ⓒworthwhile in the end. Giving up would mean acknowledging that we've wasted something we can't get back, and that thought is so ⓓpain that we prefer to avoid it if we can. The problem, of course, is that if something really is a bad bet, then staying with it simply increases the amount ⓔthat we lose. Rather than walk away from a bad five-year relationship, for example, we turn it into a bad 10-year relationship; rather than accept that we've lost a thousand dollars, we lay down another thousand and lose that too. In the end, by delaying the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.

items.passage: [25년 6월 32번] Steve Jobs used analogy to get people to embrace the new technology. Before computers, people worked in a physical world. We used paper and pens and physical file folders and so on. The idea of working in a virtual world was radically different. Or at least seemed radically different. What Jobs understood was that a physical office was fundamentally similar to a virtual office. To win over the masses, Jobs drew strong analogies between the traditional workplace people knew well with the new, unfamiliar virtual workplace. In the pre-computer workplace, when ideas were written on paper it was called ... a document. When those documents needed to be stored they were put in ... a folder. And those folders were kept on ... a desk. Documents, folders, and desktops are the terms we use in our virtual work because Steve Jobs understood that using familiar terms would make the new technology easier to understand. The parallels between the physical and virtual workplace now seem obvious.

items.passage: [25년 3월 31번] The explosion of popular music in the second half of the twentieth century as well as the global circulation and dissemination of music by the creative industries propelled a new understanding of accessibility in relation to music. Suddenly, in the 1950s, anyone could pick up spoons, a couple of pans, a second-hand guitar and start a band. This led to specific genres such as skiffle, but also, more generally, reflected a much more relaxed and inclusive attitude to music making. ⓐWhile ordinary people had always sung and made music, the popular music movement was driven by a spirit of rebellion and freedom. ⓑThis approach led to the punk movement, whose musicians even made it a condition for their music to be non-virtuosic and accessible to all in the 1970s. ⓒGroups who had been entirely excluded from music revelled in opportunities to create. ⓓAfter all, this greater music profitability enabled everyone to enjoy creating music, but the constant attempts at new genres caused auditory fatigue among the public and fueled a culture focused excessively on commercial music consumption. ⓔSuch creative freedom led to a sense of novelty and empowerment in and beyond the music sphere.

items.passage: [24년 10월 32번] All translators feel some pressure from the community of readers for whom they are doing their work. And all translators arrive at their interpretations in dialogue with other people. The English poet Alexander Pope had pretty good Greek, but when he set about translating Homer's Iliad in the early 18th century he was not on his own. He had Greek commentaries to refer to, and translations that had already been done in English, Latin, and French - and of course he had dictionaries. Translators always draw on more than one source text. Even when the scene of translation consists of just one person with a pen, paper, and the book that is being translated, or even when it is just one person translating orally for another, that person's linguistic knowledge arises from lots of other texts and other conversations. And then his or her idea of the translation's purpose will be influenced by the expectations of the person or people it is for. In both these senses every translation is a crowd translation.

items.passage: [24년 6월 31번] In 2003, British Airways announced that they would stop operating the London to New York Concorde flight as it had proven uneconomical. What happened the very next day? Sales for the route soared sharply, while there had been no changes like discounted airfare or improved cabin service. (A) Such promotional strategies, even if implemented, would likely have had only a marginal effect; the real appeal lay in passengers' recognition that the route was in short supply. This scarcity bias might seem incomprehensible at first glance, yet it could count quite significantly in shaping human judgment and perception. (B) Interestingly, when they were asked to judge all ten from scratch, the item that had just become cut of reach was suddenly rated as the most beautiful. This kind of response had been consistently observed in similar experiments involving participants with diverse attributes. In psychology, these phenomena are known as reactance: when we are deprived of an option, we suddenly deem it more desirable. (C) Professor Stephen Worchel, intrigued by this kind of irony, designed an experiment to establish its underlying mechanism. He asked students to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness – with the agreement that afterward they could keep one as a reward for their effort. Five minutes later, however, they were informed that the poster they had appraised as the third highest was no longer available.

items.passage: [24년 6월 32번] If we’ve invested in something that hasn't repaid us – be it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship – we find it very difficult to walk away. In the end, by delaying the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.

items.passage: [24년 6월 31번] We collect stamps, coins, vintage cars even when they serve no practical purpose. The post office doesn’t accept the old stamps, the banks don’t take old coins, and the vintage cars are no longer allowed on the road. These are all side issues; the attraction is that they are in short supply. In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness – with the agreement that afterward they could keep one poster as a reward for their participation. Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. The poster that was no longer available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance: when we are deprived of an option, we suddenly deem it more attractive.

[24년 9월 31번] When we get an unfavorable outcome, in some ways the last thing we want to hear is that the process was fair. If the process is fair, however, we cannot nearly as easily externalize the outcome; we got what we got "fair and square." items.passage: [25년 3월 31번] The explosion of popular music in the second half of the twentieth century as well as the global circulation and dissemination of music by the creative industries propelled a new understanding of accessibility in relation to music. Suddenly, in the 1950s, anyone could pick up spoons, a couple of pans, a second-hand guitar and start a band. This led to specific genres such as skiffle, but also, more generally, reflected a much ⓐmore relaxed and inclusive attitude to music making. While ordinary people had always sung and made music, the popular music movement was driven by a spirit of ⓑrebellion. This approach led to the punk movement, whose musicians even made it a condition for their music to be ⓒvirtuosic in the 1970s. Groups who had been entirely ⓓexcluded from music revelled in opportunities to create. This led to a sense of novelty and ⓔempowerment in and beyond the music sphere.

items.passage: [24년 10월 32번] All translators feel some pressure from the community of readers for whom they are doing their work. And all translators arrive at their interpretations in dialogue with other people. The English poet Alexander Pope had pretty good Greek, but when he set about translating Homer's Iliad in the early 18th century he was not on his own. He had Greek commentaries to refer to, and translations that had already been done in English, Latin, and French – and of course he had dictionaries. Translators always draw on more than one source text. Even when the scene of translation consists of just one person with a pen, paper, and the book that is being translated, or even when it is just one person translating orally for another, that person's linguistic knowledge arises from lots of other texts and other conversations. And then his or her idea of the translation's purpose will be influenced by the expectations of the person or people it is for. In both these senses every translation is a crowd translation.
items.stem: 다음 글의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: More Pressure Leads to Worse Results
The Secluded Life of a Translator

items.passage: [24년 6월 31번] The poster that was no longer available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. We collect stamps, coins, vintage cars even when they serve no practical purpose. The post office doesn't accept the old stamps, the banks don't take old coins, and the vintage cars are no longer allowed on the road. (A) These are all side issues; the attraction is that they are in short supply. (B) In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness – with the agreement that afterward they could keep one poster as a reward for their participation. (C) Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. (D) Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. (E) In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance: when we are deprived of an option, we suddenly deem it more attractive.

items.passage: [24년 6월 32번] If we’ve invested in something that hasn't repaid us – be it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship – we find it very difficult to walk away. This is the sunk cost fallacy. Our instinct is to continue investing money or time as we hope that our investment will prove to be ⓐworthwhile in the end. Giving up would mean acknowledging that we’ve wasted something we can’t get back, and that thought is so ⓑdistressing that we prefer to avoid it if we can. The problem, of course, is that if something really is a bad bet, then staying with it only leads to ⓒsmaller losses. Rather than walk away from a bad five-year relationship, for example, we turn it into a bad 10-year relationship; rather than accept that we’ve lost a thousand dollars, we invest another thousand and end up ⓓlosing that amount as well. In the end, by ⓔpostponing the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.

items.passage: [25년 6월 32번] Steve Jobs used analogy to get people to embrace the new technology. Before computers, people worked in a physical world. (A) To bridge this gap, Jobs drew strong analogies between the traditional workplace people knew well with the new, unfamiliar virtual workplace. In the pre-computer workplace, when ideas were written on paper it was called ... a document. When those documents needed to be stored they were put in ... a folder. And those folders were kept on ... a desk. (B) As a result, documents, folders, and desktops are the terms we use in our virtual work because Steve Jobs understood that using familiar terms would make the new technology easier to understand. The parallels between the physical and virtual workplace now seem obvious. (C) We used paper and pens and physical file folders and so on. The idea of working in a virtual world was radically different. Or at least seemed radically different. What Jobs understood was that a physical office was fundamentally similar to a virtual office.

items.passage: [25년 6월 35번] The writer and zoologist Desmond Morris observed that our feet ⓐconvey our genuine thoughts and emotions more accurately than any other part of our bodies. Why are the feet and legs such ⓑdeceptive indicators of our sentiments? For millions of years, long before humans spoke, our legs and feet reacted to environmental threats (e.g., hot sand, ill tempered lions) ⓒreflexively, without the need for conscious thought. Our limbic brains made sure that our feet and legs reacted as needed by either ceasing motion, running away, or kicking at a potential threat. This survival regimen, retained from our ancestral heritage, has ⓓsustained us to this day. In fact, these age-old reactions are still so ⓔingrained in us that when we are presented with something dangerous or even disagreeable, our feet and legs still react as they did in prehistoric times.

We don't normally see this lensing because stars aren't visible during daytime ⓒwhen the Sun is out, but a solar eclipse in 1919 enabled scientists ⓓto observe what the Sun's gravity was doing to the light from distant stars. The stars around the Sun appeared to have moved from their normal positions in the night sky. The shift was ⓔstill larger than Newton's theory predicted, but exactly in the positions predicted by Einstein's theory.

items.passage: [25년 9월 35번] A solar eclipse, however, in 1919 allowed scientists to observe what the Sun's gravity was doing to the light from distant stars. Sometimes theory comes before the data, the data distinguishing between competing theories. This is what happened when Einsteinian physics revealed the limits of Newtonian physics at the turn of the twentieth century. Newtonian physics works very well for calculating how fast a tennis ball will fall based on the angle, speed, and spin with which it's hit given the acceleration caused by the pull of the Earth's gravity. (A) Einsteinian physics taught us that the Earth's gravity isn't pulling the tennis ball at all: it's warping the fabric of space-time. (B) According to Einstein's theory, a large mass like the Sun bends space-time. Newton's theory makes no such prediction. (C) This bending of space-time leads to phenomena such as 'gravitational lensing' where the light of distant stars appears to be in different locations when they pass by a large mass like the Sun. (D) We don't normally see this lensing because stars aren't visible during the day when the Sun is out. (E) The stars around the Sun appeared to have moved from their normal positions in the night sky. The shift was much larger than Newton's theory predicted, but exactly in the positions predicted by Einstein's theory. *warp: 뒤틀다, 왜곡하다

items.passage: [25년 9월 35번] The stars around the Sun appeared to have moved from their normal positions in the night sky. According to Einstein's theory, a large mass like the Sun 'bends' space-time. Newton's theory makes no such prediction. (A) This bending of space-time leads to phenomena such as 'gravitational lensing' where the light of distant stars appears to be in different locations when they pass by a large mass like the Sun. (B) We don't normally see this lensing because stars aren't visible during the day when the Sun is out, but a solar eclipse in 1919 allowed scientists to observe what the Sun's gravity was doing to the light from distant stars. (C) This occurs because light follows the curvature of space-time near massive objects, causing its path to bend. (D) In more extreme cases, such bending can produce arcs, multiple images, or even complete rings of the same distant star or galaxy, making gravitational lensing a powerful observational tool in astronomy. (E) The shift was much larger than Newton's theory predicted, but exactly in the positions predicted by Einstein's theory.

items.passage: [24년 9월 35번] Minimal processing can be one of the best ways to keep original flavors and taste, without any need to add artificial flavoring or additives, or too much salt. This would also be the efficient way to keep most nutrients, especially the most sensitive ones such as many vitamins and anti-oxidants. Milling of cereals is one of the most harsh processes which dramatically affect nutrient content. While grains are naturally very rich in micronutrients, anti-oxidants and fiber (i.e. in wholemeal flour or flakes), milling usually removes the vast majority of minerals, vitamins and fibers to raise white flour. Such a spoilage of key nutrients and fiber is no longer acceptable in the context of a sustainable diet aiming at an optimal nutrient density and health protection. In contrast, fermentation of various foodstuffs or germination of grains are traditional, locally accessible, low-energy and highly nutritious processes of sounded interest.

the impact of the least processing on nutrient preservation
the key differences between whole wheat and refined flour
the function of anti-oxidants in strengthening the immune system

items.passage: [24년 3월 35번] We are the only species that seasons its food, deliberately altering it with the highly flavored plant parts we call herbs and spices. It's quite possible that our taste for spices has an evolutionary root. Many spices have antibacterial properties – in fact, common seasonings such as garlic, onion, and oregano inhibit the growth of almost every bacterium tested. And the cultures that make the heaviest use of spices – think of the garlic and black pepper of Thai food, the ginger and coriander of India, the chili peppers of Mexico – come from warmer climates, where bacterial spoilage is a bigger issue. In contrast, the most lightly spiced cuisines – those of Scandinavia and northern Europe – are from cooler climates. Our uniquely human attention to flavor, in this case the flavor of spices, turns out to have arisen as a matter of life and death.

spices as natural agents against bacterial growth
the human habit of modifying food from its original state
the role of spices in enhancing food safety and preservation for survival

items.passage: [25년 9월 35번] Einstein suggested that a huge mass like the Sun causes space-time to bend, an idea not addressed in Newton's system. This bending explains the effect called gravitational lensing, where light coming from distant stars seems to be displaced when it passes close to a massive body. (A) Yet, the solar eclipse of 1919 gave scientists a rare chance to observe it directly. At that moment, the stars near the Sun appeared to be shifted from their normal night-sky positions. (B) Usually, this bending of starlight goes unnoticed during the daytime, since the intense brightness of the Sun completely blocks the faint light emitted by distant stars. (C) The displacement was far greater than Newton's theory predicted, but it coincided exactly with Einstein's calculations, giving clear evidence for his revolutionary idea.

items.passage: [25년 6월 35번] The writer and zoologist Desmond Morris observed that our feet communicate exactly what we think and feel more honestly than any other part of our bodies. Why are the feet and legs such accurate reflectors of our sentiments? For millions of years, long before humans spoke, our legs and feet reacted to environmental threats (e.g., hot sand, ill-tempered lions) instantaneously, without the need for conscious thought. Our limbic brains made sure that our feet and legs reacted as needed by either ceasing motion, running away, or kicking at a potential threat. This survival regimen, retained from our ancestral heritage, has served us well and continues to do so today. In fact, these age-old reactions are still so hardwired in us that when we are presented with something dangerous or even disagreeable, our feet and legs still react as they did in prehistoric times.

Can Lower-body Training Improve Emotional Stability?
What Makes the Human Brain the Center of Decision-making?

items.passage: [24년 9월 35번] Minimal processing can be one of the best ways to keep original flavors and taste, without any need to add artificial flavoring or additives, or too much salt. This would also be the efficient way to keep most nutrients, especially the most sensitive ones such as many vitamins and anti-oxidants. Milling of cereals is one of the most harsh processes which dramatically affect nutrient content. While grains are naturally very rich in micronutrients, anti-oxidants and fiber (i.e. in wholemeal flour or flakes), milling usually removes the vast majority of minerals, vitamins and fibers to raise white flour. Such a spoilage of key nutrients and fiber is no longer acceptable in the context of a sustainable diet aiming at an optimal nutrient density and health protection.

items.passage: [24년 6월 35번] As the old joke goes: “Software, free. User manual, $10,000.” But it’s no joke. A couple of high-profile companies make their living selling instruction and paid support for free software. The copy of code, being mere bits, is free. The lines of free code become valuable to you only through support and guidance. A lot of medical and genetic information will go this route in the coming decades. Right now getting a full copy of all your DNA is very expensive ($10,000), but soon it won’t be. The price is dropping so fast, it will be $100 soon, and then the next year insurance companies will offer to sequence you for free. When a copy of your sequence costs nothing, the interpretation of what it means, what you can do about it, and how to use it – the manual for your genes – will be expensive. _________________________________________________________.

items.passage: [24년 6월 39번] Imagine that seven out of ten working Americans got fired tomorrow. What would they all do? It’s hard to believe you’d have an economy at all if you gave pink slips to more than half the labor force. (A) Since then, wave upon wave of new occupations have arrived – appliance repair person, food chemist, photographer, web designer – each building on previous automation. Today, the vast majority of us are doing jobs that no farmer from the 1800s could have imagined. (B) But that is what the industrial revolution did to the workforce of the early 19th century. Two hundred years ago, 70 percent of American workers lived on the farm. Today automation has eliminated all but 1 percent of their jobs, replacing them with machines. But the displaced workers did not sit idle. (C) Instead, automation created hundreds of millions of jobs in entirely new fields. Those who once farmed were now manning the factories that manufactured farm equipment, cars, and other industrial products.

items.passage: [25년 6월 39번] The big problem with money created by the government is that those who run the government always face the temptation to create more money and spend it. (A) For this reason, many countries have preferred using gold, silver, or some other material that is inherently limited in supply, as money. (B) This expression is misleading only if we imagine that the value of the gold is somehow transferred to the paper money, when in fact the real point is that the gold simply limits the amount of paper money that can be issued. (C) Whether among ancient kings or modern politicians, this has happened again and again over the centuries, leading to inflation and the many economic and social problems that follow from inflation. (D) It is a way of depriving governments of the power to expand the money supply to inflationary levels. Gold has long been considered ideal for this purpose, since the supply of gold in the world usually cannot be increased rapidly. (E) When paper money is convertible into gold whenever the individual chooses to do so, then the money is said to be "backed up" by gold.

items.passage: [24년 9월 39번] Sleep is clearly about more than just resting. One curious fact is that animals that are hibernating also have periods of sleep. It comes as a surprise to most of us, but hibernation and sleep are not the same thing at all, at least not from a neurological and metabolic perspective. Hibernating is more like being anesthetized: the subject is unconscious but not actually asleep. So a hibernating animal needs to get a few hours of conventional sleep each day within the larger unconsciousness. A further surprise to most of us is that bears, the most famous of wintry sleepers, don't actually hibernate. Real hibernation involves profound unconsciousness and a dramatic fall in body temperature - often to around 32 degrees Fahrenheit. By this definition, bears don't hibernate, because their body temperature stays near normal and they are easily awakened. Their winter sleeps are more accurately called a state of torpor.

items.passage: [25년 6월 38번] The word "migration" is almost always reported in the popular media and even in scientific literature as a problem or a crisis. For example, migrants are assumed to overcrowd cities, clog up labor markets, and ⓐincrease poverty. The other questionable assumption is that most migration is ⓑinvoluntary – people fleeing natural or man-made disasters. The reality, however, is more complex, and many migrants are simply seeking greater economic opportunity. Of course migration can and does ⓒcreate social and economic problems, but migration can also be a solution for many preexisting problems. For example, out-migration generally redistributes workers from places of labor surplus to areas where there is greater demand or more opportunity. Migration is generally selective of persons who are younger, healthier, more flexible, and more ⓓwilling to endure hardship in hopes of a better life relative to their prospects in their places of origin. Most research that examines long-term outcomes of migration, including remittances and intergenerational mobility, finds no ⓔpositive "long-term" effects on places of origin and destination.

items.passage: [24년 10월 39번] According to Hobbes, man is not a being who can act morally in spite of his instinct to protect his existence in the state of nature. Hence, the only place where morality and moral liberty will begin to find an application begins in a place where a sovereign power, namely the state, emerges. Hobbes thus describes the state of nature as a circumstance in which man's life is "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish and short". This means that without a central power to maintain control, people are bound to live in a constant state of _____________. In other words, Hobbes, who accepted that human beings are not social and political beings in the state of nature, believes that without the power human beings in the state of nature are "antisocial and rational based on their selfishness". Moreover, since society is not a natural phenomenon and there is no natural force bringing people together, what will bring them together as a society is not mutual affection according to Hobbes. It is, rather, mutual fear of men's present and future that assembles them, since the cause of fear is a common drive among people in the state of nature.

items.passage: [25년 6월 38번] The word "migration" is almost always reported in the popular media and even in scientific literature as a problem or a crisis. Looking at the academic literature on the media coverage of immigration, there is a consensus among scholars that migration issues are generally presented in a negative manner and as a problem increasing the sense of panic and public anxieties. Many studies have shown that migrants and refugees are framed in the media as invaders and a potential threat to the well-being of receiving citizens are not sufficiently informed to evaluate political issues directly but take their cues from the media. Thus, media reporting provides most citizens with a basic source of knowledge, serving as a link between 'cue-givers' and 'cue-recipients'. Since humans have been shown to be more interested in and more reactive to negative information, journalists apply selective and cognitive bias in the framings and interpretations of migration, which lean towards negativity, to be able to address the 'attention market'. In this vicious circle, migration issues are often pictured as economic, cultural or criminal threats - a negativity which leads to an increase in the perception that migration is more problematic relative to other policy issues.

items.passage: [25년 6월 39번] For much of history, gold has been used as money by many countries. Sometimes the gold has been used directly in coins or (for large purchases) in nuggets, gold bars or other forms. Even more convenient for carrying around were pieces of paper money printed by the government that were redeemable in gold whenever you wanted it redeemable. ⓐIt was not only more convenient to carry around paper money, but it was also safer than carrying large sums of money as mental that jingled in your pockets or was conspicuous in bags, attracting the attention of criminals. ⓑWithout paper money, it would be virtually impossible for criminals and corrupt officials to profit from illegal activities. ⓒThe big problem with money created by the government is that those who run the government always face the temptation to create more money and spend it. ⓓWhether among ancient kings or modern politicians, this has happened again and again over the centuries, leading to inflation and the many economic and social problems that flow from inflation. ⓔFor this reason, many countries have preferred using gold, silver, or some material that is inherently limited in supply, as money.

items.passage: [25년 6월 39번] The big problem with money created by the government is that those who run the government always face the temptation to create more money and spend it. Whether among ancient kings or modern politicians, this has happened again and again over the centuries, leading to inflation and the many economic and social problems that follow from inflation. For this reason, many countries have preferred using gold, silver, or some other material that is inherently limited in supply, as money. It is a way of depriving governments of the power to expand the money supply to inflationary levels. Gold has long been considered ideal for this purpose, since the supply of gold in the world usually cannot be increased rapidly. When paper money is convertible into gold whenever the individual chooses to do so, then the money is said to be "backed up" by gold. This expression is misleading only if we imagine that the value of the gold is somehow transferred to the paper money, when in fact the real point is that the gold simply limits the amount of paper money that can be issued.

items.passage: [25년 6월 38번] The word "migration" is almost always reported in the popular media and even in scientific literature as a problem or a crisis. For example, migrants are assumed to overcrowd cities, clog up labor markets, and increase poverty. The other questionable assumption is that most migration is involuntary - people fleeing natural or man-made disasters. The reality, however, is more complex, and many migrants are simply seeking greater economic opportunity. Of course migration can and does create social and economic problems. But migration can also be a solution for many preexisting problems. For example, out-migration generally redistributes workers from places of labor surplus to areas where there is greater demand or more opportunity. Migration is generally selective of persons who are younger, healthier, more flexible, and more willing to endure hardship in hopes of a better life relative to their prospects in their places of origin. Most research that examines long‐term outcomes of migration, including remittances and intergenerational mobility, finds positive "long-term" effects on places of origin and destination.

items.passage: [24년 6월 39번] Imagine that seven out of ten working Americans got fired tomorrow. What would they all do? Yet, this is essentially what happened during the industrial revolution to the early 19th-century workforce. Two hundred years ago, 70 percent of American workers lived on the farm. Today, automation has ⓐreduced that number to just 1 percent, with machines taking over most of the farming jobs. However, those displaced workers became ⓑinactive. Instead, automation led to the creation of hundreds of millions of jobs in completely ⓒnew fields. Those who once farmed were now manning the factories that manufactured farm equipment, cars, and other industrial products. Since then, new occupations have continually emerged ─ such as appliance repair technicians, food chemists, photographers, and web designers ─ all stemming from ⓓprevious waves of automation. Today, the vast majority of us are doing jobs that no farmer from the 1800s ⓔcould have imagined.

items.passage: [24년 10월 39번] According to Hobbes, man is not a being who can act morally in spite of his instinct to protect his existence in the state of nature. Hence, the only place where morality and moral liberty will begin to find an application begins in a place where a sovereign power, namely the state, emerges. Hobbes thus describes the state of nature as a circumstance in which man's life is "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish and short". It means when people live without a general power to control them all, they are indeed in a state of war. In other words, Hobbes, who accepted that human beings are not social and political beings in the state of nature, believes that without the power human beings in the state of nature are "antisocial and rational based on their selfishness". Moreover, since society is not a natural phenomenon and there is no natural force bringing people together, what will bring them together as a society is not mutual affection according to Hobbes. It is, rather, mutual fear of men's present and future that assembles them, since the cause of fear is a common drive among people in the state of nature. ⓐ Human beings in the state of nature cannot act morally because of their instinct for self-preservation. ⓑ Morality becomes possible only when a sovereign authority, namely the state, emerges to govern people. ⓒ People in the state of nature are neither social nor political, and their behavior is not driven by self-interested rationality. ⓓ Humans in the state of nature naturally cooperate with one another and build society. ⓔ Society is formed not by mutual affection but by mutual fear, which is shared by people in the state of nature. ⓕ Fear plays no significant role in forming society, since people come together voluntarily.

In reality, the key point is that gold serves as a control mechanism, capping how much paper money can be printed. items.passage: [24년 10월 39번] According to Hobbes, man is not a being who can act morally in spite of his instinct to protect his existence in the state of nature. Hence, the only place where morality and moral liberty will begin to find an application begins in a place where a sovereign power, namely the state, emerges. Hobbes thus describes the state of nature as a circumstance in which man's life is "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish and short". It means when people live without a general power to control them all, they are indeed in a state of war. In other words, Hobbes, who accepted that human beings are not social and political beings in the state of nature, believes that without the power human beings in the state of nature are "antisocial and rational based on their selfishness". Moreover, since society is not a natural phenomenon and there is no natural force bringing people together, what will bring them together as a society is not mutual affection according to Hobbes. It is, rather, mutual fear of men's present and future that assembles them, since the cause of fear is a common drive among people in the state of nature. *brutish: 잔인한 ↓ 1. Statement of Human Nature The passage begins by describing Hobbes's view of human nature: in the state of nature, man acts only according to ⓐthe instinct of self-preservation and therefore cannot behave morally. -------------------------------------------------------------- 2. Introduction of the Sovereign Power It then explains that morality and moral liberty become ⓑpossible only when a sovereign power emerges. -------------------------------------------------------------- 3. Depiction of the State of Nature Hobbes characterizes the natural condition of human life as "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short," emphasizing ⓒthe chaos and insecurity that prevail without a central authority. -------------------------------------------------------------- 4. Elaboration of Hobbes's belief Next, the author clarifies that according to Hobbes, human beings are not naturally social or political, but rather ⓓantisocial and rationally selfish in the absence of power. -------------------------------------------------------------- 5. ⓔObstacles of Social Formation Finally, the passage concludes by explaining that what unites individuals into society is not mutual affection but mutual fear, which becomes a common motivating force that compels people to establish social order.

items.passage: [25년 6월 39번] The big problem with money created by the government is that those who run the government always face the temptation to create more money and spend it. Whether among ancient kings or modern politicians, this has happened again and again over the centuries, leading to inflation and the many economic and social problems that follow from inflation. For this reason, many countries have preferred using gold, silver, or some other material that is inherently limited in supply, as money. It is a way of depriving governments of the power to expand the money supply to inflationary levels. Gold has long been considered ideal for this purpose, since the supply of gold in the world usually cannot be increased rapidly. When paper money is convertible into gold whenever the individual chooses to do so, then the money is said to be "backed up" by gold. This expression, however, deserves a cautious look as it might be misleading if people assume that the value of gold is transferred to the paper money, when in fact the real point is that _____________________________________.

the true value of money is supported by the various usages of gold
paper money loses its significance as the value of gold becomes inflated
paper money and gold circulate together to maintain economic balance
gold places a constraint on the volume of paper currency that can be issued

Today, the vast majority of us are doing jobs that no farmer from the 1800s could have imagined. ① Automation: the Driving Force Behind New Job Creation ② New Era of Machines Brought about by Industrial Revolution ③ Laid-Off Workers Caused by Collapse of Agrarian Society ④ Cost-Effective Automation Applied to Farming Industry ⑤ How to Stop Recession Due to Soaring Unemployment Rate 다음 글의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은? ⑤ [수능특강 라이트 영어독해연습 12강 6번] The need to belong is so innately human that no one can deny its importance. On some level, we all want to be accepted by others—so much so that social exclusion causes the same areas of your brain to light up that physical pain does. Think of a time when you felt that you did not belong—when you were unwelcomed, unloved, treated with suspicion, or even ignored. How did it feel? If not painful, it was most likely not a situation you would want to find yourself in again. This is part of the reason we try to hire people who are "culture fits" with our organizations. We want to avoid having people who are unhappy or quit because they don't fit in. While this strategy may help maintain harmony, it can also unintentionally limit diversity and fresh perspectives within a team. In the long run, fostering an inclusive environment where a range of different viewpoints can coexist may be more beneficial for the growth and innovation of the organization.

items.passage: [24년 6월 39번] Imagine that seven out of ten working Americans got fired tomorrow. What would they all do? It's hard to believe you'd have an economy at all if you gave pink slips to more than half the labor force. (A) But that is what the industrial revolution did to the workforce of the early 19th century. Two hundred years ago, 70 percent of American workers lived on the farm. Today automation has eliminated all but 1 percent of their jobs, replacing them with machines. (B) Since then, wave upon wave of new occupations have arrived – appliance repair person, food chemist, photographer, web designer – each building on previous automation. Today, the vast majority of us are doing jobs that no farmer from the 1800s could have imagined. (C) However, the displaced workers did not sit idle. Instead, automation created hundreds of millions of jobs in entirely new fields. Those who once farmed were now manning the factories that manufactured farm equipment, cars, and other industrial products. *pink slip: 해고 통지서

items.passage: [25년 6월 39번] The big problem with money created by the government is that those who run the government always face the temptation to create more money and spend it. Whether among ancient kings or modern politicians, this has happened again and again over the centuries, leading to inflation and the many economic and social problems that follow from inflation. For this reason, many countries have preferred using gold, silver, or some other material that is inherently limited in supply, as money. It is a way of depriving governments of the power to expand the money supply to inflationary levels. Gold has long been considered ideal for this purpose, since the supply of gold in the world usually cannot be increased rapidly. When paper money is convertible into gold whenever the individual chooses to do so, then the money is said to be "backed up" by gold. This expression is misleading only if we imagine that the value of the gold is somehow transferred to the paper money, when in fact the real point is that the gold simply limits the amount of paper money that can be issued.
items.stem: 다음 글에서 밑줄 친 this purpose가 의미하는 바로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: leading officials to reinforce monetary policies
blocking political leaders from manipulating the supply of gold
preventing governments from inflating the economy by gold monopoly
tempting the government to issue paper money whenever they want
restricting government's authority to overproduce money and trigger inflation

items.passage: [24년 6월 38번] The Net differs from most of the mass media it replaces in an obvious and very important way: it’s bidirectional. We can send messages through the network as well as receive them, which has made the system all the more useful. The ability to exchange information online, to upload as well as download, has turned the Net into a thoroughfare for business and commerce. With a few clicks, people can search virtual catalogues, place orders, track shipments, and update information in corporate databases. But the Net doesn’t just connect us with businesses; it connects us with one another. It’s a personal broadcasting medium as well as a commercial one. Millions of people use it to distribute their own digital creations, in the form of blogs, videos, photos, songs, and podcasts, as well as to critique, edit, or otherwise modify the creations of others.

items.passage: 다음 글의 내용을 바탕으로 migration의 부정적인 면과 긍정적인 면을 아래와 같이 분류할 때 ⓐ~ⓔ 중 적절하지 않은 것은? [25년 6월 38번] The word "migration" is almost always reported in the popular media and even in scientific literature as a problem or a crisis. For example, migrants are assumed to overcrowd cities, clog up labor markets, and increase poverty. The other questionable assumption is that most migration is involuntary - people fleeing natural or man-made disasters. The reality, however, is more complex, and many migrants are simply seeking greater economic opportunity. Of course migration can and does create social and economic problems. But migration can also be a solution for many preexisting problems. For example, out-migration generally redistributes workers from places of labor surplus to areas where there is greater demand or more opportunity. Migration is generally selective of persons who are younger, healthier, more flexible, and more willing to endure hardship in hopes of a better life relative to their prospects in their places of origin. Most research that examines long-term outcomes of migration, including remittances and inter-generational mobility, finds positive "long-term" effects on places of origin and destination. negative aspect (부정적인 면)ⓐoversupplying the workforce in labor markets ⓑincreasing poverty and cities undergoing overcrowdingpositive aspect (긍정적인 면)ⓒredistribution of labor surplus ⓓyounger persons' willingness to endure hardship back in their home countries ⓔremittances and inter-generational mobility as long-term outcomes

items.passage: [24년 10월 39번] Hobbes argues that man cannot stay ⓐpeaceful in the state of nature, since his instinct to protect his existence prevents him from acting morally. Hence, the only place where morality and moral liberty will begin to find an application begins in a place in which a sovereign power, namely the state, emerges. Hobbes thus describes the state of nature as a circumstance ⓑwhere man's life is "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish and short". It means when people live without a general power to control them all, they are indeed in a state of war. In other words, Hobbes, who accepted that human beings are not social and political beings in the state of nature, ⓒmaintaining that without the power human beings in the state of nature are "antisocial and rational based on their selfishness". Moreover, since society is not a natural phenomenon and there is no natural force ⓓbringing people together, what will bring them together as a society is not mutual affection according to Hobbes. It is, rather, mutual fear of men's present and future that ⓔassembles them, since the cause of fear is a common drive among people in the state of nature.

items.passage: [25년 6월 38번] The word "migration" is almost always reported in the popular media and even in scientific literature as a problem or a crisis. (A) Of course migration can and does create social and economic problems. But migration can also be a solution for many preexisting problems. For example, out-migration generally redistributes workers from places of labor surplus to areas where there is greater demand or more opportunity. (B) Migration is generally selective of persons who are younger, healthier, more flexible, and more willing to endure hardship in hopes of a better life relative to their prospects in their places of origin. Most research that examines long-term outcomes of migration, including remittances and intergenerational mobility, finds positive "long-term" effects on places of origin and destination. (C) For example, migrants are assumed to overcrowd cities, clog up labor markets, and increase poverty. The other questionable assumption is that most migration is involuntary ─ people fleeing natural or man-made disasters. The reality, however, is more complex, and many migrants are simply seeking greater economic opportunity.

items.passage: [24년 6월 39번] Imagine that seven out of ten working Americans got fired tomorrow. What would they all do? It’s hard to believe you’d have an economy at all if you gave pink slips to more than half the labor force. (A) Since then, wave upon wave of new occupations have arrived – appliance repair person, food chemist, photographer, web designer – each building on previous automation. Today, the vast majority of us are doing jobs that no farmer from the 1800s could have imagined. (B) But that is what the industrial revolution did to the workforce of the early 19th century. Two hundred years ago, 70 percent of American workers lived on the farm. Today automation has eliminated all but 1 percent of their jobs, replacing them with machines. (C) But the displaced workers did not sit idle. Instead, automation created hundreds of millions of jobs in entirely new fields. Those who once farmed were now manning the factories that manufactured farm equipment, cars, and other industrial products. *pink slip: 해고 통지서

items.passage: [24년 6월 38번] The Net differs from most of the mass media it replaces in an obvious and very important way: it’s bidirectional. We can send messages through the network as well as receive them, which has made the system all the more useful. The ability to exchange information online, to upload as well as download, has turned the Net into a thoroughfare for business and commerce. With a few clicks, people can search virtual catalogues, place orders, track shipments, and update information in corporate databases. But the Net doesn’t just connect us with businesses; it connects us with one another. It’s a personal broadcasting medium as well as a commercial one. Millions of people use it to distribute their own digital creations, in the form of blogs, videos, photos, songs, and podcasts, as well as to critique, edit, or otherwise modify the creations of others. *bidirectional: 두 방향으로 작용하는 **thoroughfare: 통로

items.passage: [25년 6월 39번] The big problem with money created by the government is that those who run the government always face the temptation to create more money and spend it. Whether among ancient kings or modern politicians, this has happened again and again over the centuries, leading to inflation and the many economic and social problems that follow from inflation. ⓐBut low inflation and a "stable economy have softened demand for gold, sending its price tumbling. ⓑFor this reason, many countries have preferred using gold, silver, or some other material that is inherently limited in supply, as money. ⓒIt is a way of depriving governments of the power to expand the money supply to inflationary levels. ⓓGold has long been considered ideal for this purpose, since the supply of gold in the world usually cannot be increased rapidly. ⓔWhen paper money is convertible into gold whenever the individual chooses to do so, then the money is said to be "backed up" by gold. This expression is misleading only if we imagine that the value of the gold is somehow transferred to the paper money, when in fact the real point is that the gold simply limits the amount of paper money that can be issued.
