items.passage: [24년 9월 39번] Sleep clearly serves purposes beyond simple rest. Interestingly, even animals that hibernate experience periods of sleep. Many people find it surprising that hibernation and sleep are entirely different processes, at least from neurological and metabolic viewpoints. ⓐHibernation resembles anesthesia more than ordinary sleep: the animal is unconscious but not truly asleep. ⓑSome studies have also explored whether short naps during the day can boost human productivity and memory retention. ⓒThis is why a hibernating animal still requires several hours of regular sleep each day within its extended state of unconsciousness. ⓓAnother fact that often surprises people is that bears – the animals most commonly associated with winter sleep – don't actually hibernate. ⓔBears aren't real hibernators because their body temperature remains relatively normal and they can be awakened easily.

items.passage: [24년 6월 39번] Imagine that seven out of ten working Americans got fired tomorrow. What would they all do? It's hard to believe you'd have an economy at all if you gave pink slips to more than half the labor force. But that is what the industrial revolution did to the workforce of the early 19th century. ⓐAbout two centuries ago, 70 percent of workers America made their living on farms. ⓑTheir unemployment caused by a lack of agricultural skills was inevitable at the time. ⓒNowadays, automation has reduced those farming jobs to just 1 percent, with machines taking over nearly all of the work. ⓓYet, those who were displaced from their jobs didn't simply stay idle. ⓔOn the contrary, automation led to the emergence of hundreds of millions of jobs in brand new industries. Those who once farmed were now manning the factories that manufactured farm equipment, cars, and other industrial products. Since then, wave upon wave of new occupations have arrived ─ appliance repair person, food chemist, photographer, web designer ─ each building on previous automation. Today, the vast majority of us are doing jobs that no farmer from the 1800s could have imagined.

items.passage: [24년 9월 39번] Sleep clearly serves purposes beyond simple rest. Interestingly, even animals that hibernate experience periods of sleep. Many people find it surprising that hibernation and sleep are entirely different processes, at least from neurological and metabolic viewpoints. ⓐHibernation resembles anesthesia more than ordinary sleep: the animal is unconscious but not truly asleep. ⓑSome studies have also explored whether short naps during the day can boost human productivity and memory retention. ⓒThis is why a hibernating animal still requires several hours of regular sleep each day within its extended state of unconsciousness. ⓓAnother fact that often surprises people is that bears – the animals most commonly associated with winter sleep – don't actually hibernate. ⓔBears aren't real hibernators because their body temperature remains relatively normal and they can be awakened easily.

items.passage: [25년 6월 39번] The big problem with money created by the government is that those who run the government always face the temptation to create more money and spend it. Whether among ancient kings or modern politicians, this has happened again and again over the centuries, leading to inflation and the many economic and social problems that follow from inflation. For this reason, many countries have preferred using gold, silver, or some other material that is inherently limited in supply, as money. It is a way of depriving governments of the power to expand the money supply to inflationary levels. Gold has long been considered ideal for this purpose, since the supply of gold in the world usually cannot be increased rapidly. When paper money is convertible into gold whenever the individual chooses to do so, then the money is said to be (A)"backed up" by gold. This expression is misleading only if we imagine that the value of the gold is somehow transferred to the paper money, when in fact the real point is that the gold simply limits the amount of paper money that can be issued.

items.passage: [25년 6월 38번] The word "migration" is almost always reported in the popular media and even in scientific literature as a problem or a crisis. For example, migrants are assumed to overcrowd cities, clog up labor markets, and increase poverty. The other questionable assumption is that most migration is ⓐforced ─ people fleeing natural or man-made disasters. The reality, however, is more complex, and many migrants are simply seeking greater economic opportunity. Of course migration can and does create social and economic ⓑchallenges. But migration can also be a solution for many preexisting problems. For example, out-migration generally ⓒreallocates workers from places of labor surplus to areas where there is greater demand or more opportunity. Migration is generally selective of persons who are younger, healthier, more flexible, and more ⓓinclined to endure hardship in hopes of a better life relative to their prospects in their places of origin. Most research that examines long‐term outcomes of migration, including remittances and intergenerational mobility, finds ⓔunfavorable "long-term" effects on places of origin and destination.

items.passage: [24년 10월 39번] According to Hobbes, man is not a being who can act morally in spite of his instinct to protect his existence in the state of nature. Hence, the only place where morality and moral liberty will begin to find an application begins in a place where a sovereign power, namely the state, emerges. Hobbes thus describes the state of nature as a circumstance in which man's life is "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish and short". It means when people live without a general power to control them all, they are indeed in a state of war. In other words, Hobbes, who accepted that human beings are not social and political beings in the state of nature, believes that without the power human beings in the state of nature are "antisocial and rational based on their selfishness". Moreover, since society is not a natural phenomenon and there is no natural force bringing people together, what will bring them together as a society is not mutual affection according to Hobbes. It is, rather, mutual fear of men's present and future that assembles them, since the cause of fear is a common drive among people in the state of nature. ⓐ In the state of nature, humans can act morally by overcoming their instinct for self-preservation. ⓑ Morality and moral liberty only begin to apply once a sovereign power, such as the state, is established. ⓒ The state of nature is essentially a state of war because there is no general power to control everyone. ⓓ Human beings are inherently social and political creatures who naturally seek to form communities. ⓔ Humans in the state of nature are always antisocial and selfish regardless of the presence of power. ⓕ Society is a natural phenomenon brought together by the mutual affection people feel for one another. ⓖ What assembles people into a society is mutual fear of their present and future.

Millions of people use it to distribute their own digital creations, in the form of blogs, videos, photos, songs, and podcasts, as well as to critique, edit, or otherwise modify the creations of others.

items.passage: [25년 6월 38번] For example, it helps to balance labor markets by moving workers from areas with a surplus to those with greater demand. Migration is often portrayed in the media and academic literature as a problem or crisis, with concerns about overcrowded cities, strained labor markets, and increased poverty. (A) However, the reality is much more nuanced; while it is commonly assumed that most migration is forced by disasters, many people actually move voluntarily in search of better economic prospects. (B) Migration can indeed bring about social and economic challenges, but it also has the potential to address existing issues. (C) Migrants are typically younger, healthier, and more adaptable, seeking improved opportunities compared to what is available in their home regions. (D) Moreover, research into the long-term impacts of migration, such as remittances and upward mobility across generations, generally finds that migration produces positive outcomes for both the places migrants leave and the places they settle. (E) In summary, migration is not simply a crisis; it is a complex phenomenon that can contribute positively to society and the economy over time.

items.passage: [25년 6월 39번] The big problem with money created by the government is that those who run the government always face the temptation to create more money and spend it. Whether among ancient kings or modern politicians, this has happened again and again over the centuries, leading to inflation and the many economic and social problems that follow from inflation. For this reason, many countries have preferred using gold, silver, or some other material that is inherently limited in supply, as money. It is a way of depriving governments of the power to expand the money supply to inflationary levels. Gold has long been considered ideal for this purpose, since ____________________________________________. When paper money is convertible into gold whenever the individual chooses to do so, then the money is said to be "backed up" by gold. This expression is misleading only if we imagine that the value of the gold is somehow transferred to the paper money, when in fact the real point is that the gold simply limits the amount of paper money that can be issued. ① it is the most abundant metal found in nature ② it can be converted into paper money at any time ③ it does not corrode and is widely accepted as valuable ④ it can be produced in unlimited quantities to support the economy ⑤ its global supply is naturally limited and cannot be quickly expanded 다음 글에서 전체 흐름과 관계없는 문장은? ④ [올림포스 기출 unit1-3] I am writing to inform you of an ongoing noise issue that I am experiencing. My apartment faces the basketball courts of the community center. ⓐWhile I fully support the community center's services, I am constantly being disrupted by individuals playing basketball late at night. ⓑMany nights, I struggle to fall asleep because I can hear people bouncing balls and shouting on the basketball courts well after 11 p.m. ⓒCould you restrict the time the basketball court is open to before 9 p.m.?ⓓI understand physical activity is important for community health, and I hope the basketball courts continue to inspire young athletes. ⓔI'm sure I'm not the only person in the neighborhood affected by this noise issue, and I appreciate your assistance.

items.passage: [25년 9월 41~42번] Many animals pursue a mixed strategy of accumulating both body fat and food, which leads one to ask, "What are the relative advantages and disadvantages of these two forms of energy storage?" (A) Maximum fat deposition increases with body mass whereas maximum food storage is not constrained by body size. This means animals, especially small animals, can accumulate much greater energy reserves in the form of stored food than they can in the form of body fat. Further, stored food is more economical than body fat because fat contributes to body mass, and metabolic rate increases with body mass. (B) Many animals must expend energy managing and protecting their food stores. Eating food and converting it to fat avoids these types of losses and the energetic costs of managing stored food. A large accumulation of body fat adds to an animal's fasting capacity, especially large animals, permitting some animals to enter prolonged dormancy in the relative security of a hibernaculum. (C) Excessive fat accumulations may also have a negative effect on an animal's ability to avoid predators. And, if maintaining a high body temperature is advantageous, animals might be expected to accumulate more energy in the form of a food store than as body fat. On the other hand, stored food may rot over time, may be removed by robbers, or may simply be lost. *dormancy: 휴면 상태 **hibernaculum: 동면 장소

items.passage: [25년 9월 41~42번] Many animals use both fat and food as energy reserves. Fat, stored within the body, ⓐincreasing with body size, while food storage is not limited by mass. Thus, small animals are able to secure larger reserves through food than fat. Moreover, food storage tend to reduce metabolic costs ⓑto conserve energy more efficiently, since fat increases body weight and the added weight results in a higher metabolism. Interestingly, excessive fat can hinder an animal's speed, making it ⓒharder to escape predators. In addition, if keeping warm is required, animals may prefer storing food rather than accumulating fat. However, food can spoil, be stolen, or disappear, and animals often spend energy guarding it. Fat, ⓓwhich is not subject to these risks, is converted directly from food and allows animals to survive long periods without eating. Especially in large animals, heavy fat deposits enable hibernation in safe shelters. Therefore, both strategies – fat accumulation and food storage – ⓔoffer clear benefits, depending on circumstances.

Further, stored food is more economical than body fat because fat contributes to body mass, and metabolic rate increases with body mass. In other words, there is a metabolic expense to maintaining fat. Excessive fat accumulations may also have a negative effect on an animal's ability to avoid predators. And, if maintaining a high body temperature is advantageous, animals might be expected to accumulate more energy in the form of a food store than as body fat. On the other hand, stored food may rot over time, may be removed by robbers, or may simply be lost. Many animals must expend energy managing and protecting their food stores. options: 식량 저장: 신진대사 비용이 드는 체지방 축적보다 더 경제적이다
식량 저장: 체지방의 축적보다 더 많은 에너지의 저장이 가능하다
식량 저장: 신체 크기에 제한받지 않으므로 작은 동물들에게 이롭다
체지방 축적: 많이 축적하면 할수록 포식자를 피하는 데 유리하다
체지방 축적: 부패, 도난, 분실의 우려가 없고, 일부 동물들에게 장기간 휴면(수면)을 가능하게 한다

items.passage: We might forget an anecdote about a stranger because it makes few connections with our existing associations.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 단어와 의미가 가장 가까운 것은?
items.explain: anecdote는 '일화·짧은 이야기' = short story. ① antidote(해독제)는 철자가 비슷한 함정, ④ rumor는 본문의 gossip과 연결되는 함정.

items.passage: Steve Jobs used analogy to get people to embrace the new technology.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 단어와 의미가 가장 가까운 것은?
items.explain: embrace는 '(기꺼이) 받아들이다' = accept. 반의어 ① reject가 최대 함정.

items.passage: the psychological tendency to want something more strongly when we are deprived of it or when our freedom to choose it is taken away
items.stem: 다음 영영 풀이(English definition)에 해당하는 단어는?
items.explain: '선택지를 빼앗기면 더 원하게 되는 심리'의 정의 → reactance. ③ scarcity(희소성)는 원인이지 현상명이 아니다.

items.passage: The most effective communicators find ways to make the abstract personal.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 단어의 반의어로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: abstract(추상적)의 반의어 = concrete(구체적). 본문에서 abstract ↔ personal/concrete 대립.

items.passage: If the process is fair, we cannot nearly as easily externalize the outcome.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 단어의 반의어로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: externalize(외부화)의 반의어 = internalize(내면화). 나머지 -ize 단어는 의미가 무관한 함정.

items.passage: The idea of working in a virtual world was radically different.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 단어의 반의어로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: virtual(가상의)의 반의어 = physical(물리적·실물의). ④ imaginary는 virtual의 유의어에 가까워 반의어가 아니다.

items.passage: The explosion of popular music in the second half of the twentieth century as well as the global circulation and dissemination of music by the creative industries propelled a new understanding of accessibility in relation to music. Suddenly, in the 1950s, anyone could pick up spoons, a couple of pans, a second-hand guitar and start a band. This led to specific genres such as skiffle, but also, more generally, reflected a much more relaxed and inclusive attitude to music making. While ordinary people had always sung and made music, the popular music movement was driven by a spirit of rebellion and freedom. This approach led to the punk movement, whose musicians even made it a condition for their music to be non-virtuosic and accessible to all in the 1970s. Groups who had been entirely excluded from music revelled in opportunities to create. This led to a sense of novelty and empowerment in and beyond the music sphere.
items.stem: 대중음악과 접근성에 관한 글의 내용과 일치하는 것은?
options: Punk musicians insisted that their music be highly virtuosic.
Ordinary people had never sung or made music before the popular music movement.
The popular music movement was driven by a spirit of obedience.
In the 1950s, anyone could pick up everyday objects and a second-hand guitar and start a band.
Groups that had been excluded from music avoided opportunities to create.
items.explain: 본문과 일치(누구나 일상 도구·중고 기타로 밴드 시작). ① 펑크는 비기교적을 조건으로 삼아 반대, ② 보통 사람들은 늘 노래해 왔으므로 반대, ③ 저항(rebellion)이라 obedience는 반대, ⑤ 만끽했으므로 avoided는 반대.

items.passage: We collect stamps, coins, vintage cars even when they serve no practical purpose. The post office doesn't accept the old stamps, the banks don't take old coins, and the vintage cars are no longer allowed on the road. These are all side issues; the attraction is that they are in short supply. In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness ─ with the agreement that afterward they could keep one poster as a reward for their participation. Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. The poster that was no longer available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance: when we are deprived of an option, we suddenly deem it more attractive.
items.stem: 포스터 실험(리액턴스)의 내용과 일치하는 것은?
options: The poster with the highest rating became unavailable.
Students were promised that they could keep all the posters.
Students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness.
After the change, students judged only the remaining nine posters.
The unavailable poster was rated the least beautiful.
items.explain: 본문과 일치(포스터 10장을 매력도 순으로 배열). ① 사라진 건 1위가 아니라 3위, ② 보상은 1장, ④ 10장 전부 from scratch 재평가, ⑤ 사라진 포스터는 가장 아름다운 것으로 분류.

items.passage: If we've invested in something that hasn't repaid us ─ be it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship ─ we find it very difficult to walk away. This is the sunk cost fallacy. Our instinct is to continue investing money or time as we hope that our investment will prove to be worthwhile in the end. Giving up would mean acknowledging that we've wasted something we can't get back, and that thought is so painful that we prefer to avoid it if we can. The problem, of course, is that if something really is a bad bet, then staying with it simply increases the amount we lose. Rather than walk away from a bad five-year relationship, for example, we turn it into a bad 10-year relationship; rather than accept that we've lost a thousand dollars, we lay down another thousand and lose that too. In the end, by delaying the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.
items.stem: 매몰 비용 오류에 관한 글의 내용과 일치하지 않는 것은?
options: People find it very difficult to walk away from investments that have not repaid them.
Giving up would mean acknowledging that we have wasted something we cannot get back.
Staying with a genuinely bad bet simply increases the amount we lose.
The writer advises us to keep investing until it finally proves worthwhile.
Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.
items.explain: 본문은 '손실을 끊어내라'고 주장. ④ '가치 있다 판명될 때까지 계속 투자하라'는 정반대(매몰비용 오류) → 불일치. 나머지는 본문 진술.

items.passage: So if a new piece of information (A)(has / will have) something to do with us, it (B)(will be processed / will process) more easily and (C)(thoroughly / thorough).
items.stem: (A), (B), (C)의 각 네모 안에서 어법에 맞는 표현끼리 가장 적절하게 짝지은 것은?
options: will have — will be processed — thorough
items.explain: (A) 조건 if절은 미래라도 현재시제 → has. (B) 정보가 '처리되는' 수동 → will be processed. (C) 동사 수식 부사 → thoroughly.

items.passage: Steve Jobs used analogy to get people ＿＿＿＿ the new technology.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 어법상 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: get은 준사역동사로 get + O + to V. make/have/let(원형)과 달리 to부정사 → to embrace. ① embrace(원형)는 함정.

items.passage: All translators feel some pressure from the community of readers ＿＿＿＿ they are doing their work.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 어법상 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: do their work for the readers의 for가 관계사 앞으로 이동, 뒤가 완전한 절 → 전치사+관계대명사 for whom. ① for who 불가, ③ whom 단독은 전치사 누락.

items.passage: We might forget an anecdote about a stranger because it makes few connections with our existing associations, but we won't forget a piece of gossip about our cousin. There's one complex network that is larger and quicker to access than all others ─ the self. We've been thinking about ourselves in our whole lives. So if a new piece of information has something to do with us, it will be more easily and thoroughly processed. It hits even closer to home than our actual home ─ we can take a vacation away from our home, but not from ourselves. The most effective communicators find ways to make the abstract personal. Consider the warning that law schools give to motivate first-year law students concerning the rigors of their program. Hearing that "the first-year dropout rate is 33%" is an abstract statistic. "Look to your left, look to your right. One of the three of you won't be joining us next fall" wakes up the self.
items.stem: 자아와 기억에 관한 글의 주제로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: why people quickly forget gossip about their relatives
the importance of memorizing statistics accurately
how effective communicators make abstract ideas personal to engage the self
the difficulty of taking a vacation away from home
practical ways to lower the dropout rate of law schools
items.explain: 주제 = '효과적인 의사전달자는 추상을 개인화해 자아를 일깨운다'. 나머지는 세부 소재를 과장한 함정.

items.passage: If we've invested in something that hasn't repaid us ─ be it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship ─ we find it very difficult to walk away. This is the sunk cost fallacy. Our instinct is to continue investing money or time as we hope that our investment will prove to be worthwhile in the end. Giving up would mean acknowledging that we've wasted something we can't get back, and that thought is so painful that we prefer to avoid it if we can. The problem, of course, is that if something really is a bad bet, then staying with it simply increases the amount we lose. Rather than walk away from a bad five-year relationship, for example, we turn it into a bad 10-year relationship; rather than accept that we've lost a thousand dollars, we lay down another thousand and lose that too. In the end, by delaying the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.
items.stem: 매몰 비용 오류에 관한 글의 요지로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: We should sometimes stop and accept our losses rather than chase a bad investment.
Persistence in any investment always pays off in the end.
Emotional relationships are always more valuable than financial ones.
Admitting our mistakes is unnecessary as long as we keep trying.
The longer we hold an investment, the more worthwhile it becomes.
items.explain: 요지 = '나쁜 투자를 쫓지 말고 손실을 인정·단절하라'. ②⑤는 반대, ③④는 과장·왜곡.

items.passage: Steve Jobs used analogy to get people to embrace the new technology. Before computers, people worked in a physical world. We used paper and pens and physical file folders and so on. The idea of working in a virtual world was radically different. Or at least seemed radically different. What Jobs understood was that a physical office was fundamentally similar to a virtual office. To win over the masses, Jobs drew strong analogies between the traditional workplace people knew well with the new, unfamiliar virtual workplace. In the pre-computer workplace, when ideas were written on paper it was called ... a document. When those documents needed to be stored they were put in ... a folder. And those folders were kept on ... a desk. Documents, folders, and desktops are the terms we use in our virtual work because Steve Jobs understood that using familiar terms would make the new technology easier to understand. The parallels between the physical and virtual workplace now seem obvious.
items.stem: Steve Jobs의 유추에 관한 글의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: The Failure of the Virtual Workplace
Why Physical Offices Will Never Disappear
A Short History of Paper and Pens
Steve Jobs's Long Struggle Against Analogy
Familiar Terms Open the Door to New Technology
items.explain: 친숙한 용어(document·folder·desktop)로 신기술 수용을 이끔 → 'Familiar Terms Open the Door to New Technology'.

items.passage: When we receive an unfavorable outcome, in some ways the very last thing we want to hear is that the process was fair. As outraging as the combination of an unfavorable outcome and an unfair process is, this combination also brings with it a consolation prize: the possibility of attributing the bad outcome to something other than ourselves. We may reassure ourselves by believing that our bad outcome had little to do with us and everything to do with the unfair process. If the process is fair, however, we cannot nearly as easily externalize the outcome; we got what we got "fair and square." When the process is fair we believe that our outcome is deserved, which is another way of saying that there must have been something about ourselves (what we did or who we are) that caused the outcome.
items.stem: 공정한 과정의 역설에 관한 글의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: The Hidden Cost of a Fair Process
Winning Fair and Square Brings Pure Joy
Always Blame Yourself for Every Outcome
items.explain: 공정한 과정이 결과 외부화를 막아 수용을 어렵게 하는 역설 → 'The Hidden Cost of a Fair Process'. ③은 정반대.

items.passage: The explosion of popular music in the second half of the twentieth century as well as the global circulation and dissemination of music by the creative industries propelled a new understanding of accessibility in relation to music. Suddenly, in the 1950s, anyone could pick up spoons, a couple of pans, a second-hand guitar and start a band. This led to specific genres such as skiffle, but also, more generally, reflected a much more relaxed and inclusive attitude to music making. While ordinary people had always sung and made music, the popular music movement was driven by a spirit of rebellion and freedom. This approach led to the punk movement, whose musicians even made it a condition for their music to be non-virtuosic and accessible to all in the 1970s. Groups who had been entirely excluded from music revelled in opportunities to create. This led to a sense of novelty and empowerment in and beyond the music sphere.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 This가 가리키는 것으로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: the global circulation of music by the creative industries
the punk movement that emerged in the 1970s
the spirit of rebellion and freedom
the fact that, in the 1950s, anyone could use everyday objects to start a band
a sense of novelty and empowerment
items.explain: This는 앞 문장(1950년대 누구나 일상 도구로 밴드 시작)을 가리킴. ② 펑크는 This의 결과(뒤 문장)라 함정.
items.lead: This led to specific genres such as skiffle, but also, more generally, reflected a much more relaxed and inclusive attitude to music making.

items.passage: If we've invested in something that hasn't repaid us ─ be it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship ─ we find it very difficult to walk away. This is the sunk cost fallacy. Our instinct is to continue investing money or time as we hope that our investment will prove to be worthwhile in the end. Giving up would mean acknowledging that we've wasted something we can't get back, and that thought is so painful that we prefer to avoid it if we can. The problem, of course, is that if something really is a bad bet, then staying with it simply increases the amount we lose. Rather than walk away from a bad five-year relationship, for example, we turn it into a bad 10-year relationship; rather than accept that we've lost a thousand dollars, we lay down another thousand and lose that too. In the end, by delaying the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 it이 가리키는 것으로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: add to it의 it은 앞의 the pain. ② our problem은 지칭 함정 — 미루는 것은 '고통'이지 '문제'가 아니다.
items.lead: In the end, by delaying the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it.

items.passage: We might forget an anecdote about a stranger because it makes few connections with our existing associations, but we won't forget a piece of gossip about our cousin. There's one complex network that is larger and quicker to access than all others ─ the self. We've been thinking about ourselves in our whole lives. So if a new piece of information has something to do with us, it will be more easily and thoroughly processed. It hits even closer to home than our actual home ─ we can take a vacation away from our home, but not from ourselves. The most effective communicators find ways to make the abstract personal. Consider the warning that law schools give to motivate first-year law students concerning the rigors of their program. Hearing that "the first-year dropout rate is 33%" is an abstract statistic. "Look to your left, look to your right. One of the three of you won't be joining us next fall" wakes up the self.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 연결어로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 앞(평생 자신을 생각)의 결과로 뒤(나와 관련된 정보는 더 잘 처리)가 이어짐 → 인과·결과의 So.
items.lead: We have been thinking about ourselves in our whole lives. ＿＿＿＿ if a new piece of information has something to do with us, it will be more easily and thoroughly processed.

We may reassure ourselves by believing that our bad outcome had little to do with us and everything to do with the unfair process. If the process is fair, however, we cannot nearly as easily externalize the outcome; we got what we got "fair and square." When the process is fair we believe that our outcome is deserved, which is another way of saying that there must have been something about ourselves (what we did or who we are) that caused the outcome.

items.lead: We may reassure ourselves by believing that our bad outcome had everything to do with the unfair process. If the process is fair, ＿＿＿＿, we cannot nearly as easily externalize the outcome.

Groups who had been entirely (d)excluded from music revelled in opportunities to create, leading to a sense of (e)novelty and empowerment.
items.stem: 다음 글에서 밑줄 친 (a)~(e) 중 문맥상 낱말의 쓰임이 적절하지 않은 것은?
items.explain: (c) exclusive(배타적)가 문맥상 틀림 → inclusive(포용적)이어야. 저항·자유·모두에게 열린 흐름상 (a)rebellion·(b)accessible·(d)excluded·(e)novelty는 적절.

Our instinct is to continue investing as we hope our investment will prove to be (c)worthwhile. But if something really is a bad bet, staying with it simply (d)reduces the amount we lose. items.passage: to make use of a supply of something, such as knowledge or resources, that is available to you

items.explain: the agreement 뒤는 완전한 절이므로 관계사 (b)which가 아니라 동격 that이 되어야 한다. 나머지는 적절.

The problem is that if something really is a bad bet, then (b)staying with it simply (c)increase the amount we lose. items.passage: We collect stamps, coins, vintage cars even when they serve no practical purpose. The post office doesn't accept the old stamps, the banks don't take old coins, and the vintage cars are no longer allowed on the road. These are all side issues; the attraction is that they are in short supply. In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness ─ with the agreement that afterward they could keep one poster as a reward for their participation. Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. The poster that was no longer available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance: when we are deprived of an option, we suddenly deem it more attractive.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 수집품의 매력은 '공급이 부족하다'는 데 있다(주제문). ② widely available은 정반대.
items.lead: These are all side issues; the attraction is that they are ＿＿＿＿.

items.lead: The explosion of popular music in the second half of the twentieth century propelled a new understanding of ＿＿＿＿ in relation to music.

We may reassure ourselves by believing that our bad outcome had little to do with us and everything to do with the unfair process. If the process is fair, however, we cannot nearly as easily externalize the outcome; we got what we got "fair and square." items.passage: Steve Jobs used analogy to get people to embrace the new technology. Before computers, people worked in a physical world. We used paper and pens and physical file folders and so on. The idea of working in a virtual world was radically different. Or at least seemed radically different. What Jobs understood was that a physical office was fundamentally similar to a virtual office. To win over the masses, Jobs drew strong analogies between the traditional workplace people knew well with the new, unfamiliar virtual workplace. In the pre-computer workplace, when ideas were written on paper it was called ... a document. When those documents needed to be stored they were put in ... a folder. And those folders were kept on ... a desk. Documents, folders, and desktops are the terms we use in our virtual work because Steve Jobs understood that using familiar terms would make the new technology easier to understand. The parallels between the physical and virtual workplace now seem obvious.

items.explain: 친숙한 용어가 신기술 이해를 쉽게 만든다(요지 직결).
items.lead: Steve Jobs understood that using ＿＿＿＿ would make the new technology easier to understand.

In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness ─ with the agreement that afterward they could keep one poster as a reward for their participation. Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. The poster that was no longer available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. items.passage: All translators feel some pressure from the community of readers for whom they are doing their work. And all translators arrive at their interpretations in dialogue with other people. The English poet Alexander Pope had pretty good Greek, but when he set about translating Homer's Iliad in the early 18th century he was not on his own. He had Greek commentaries to refer to, and translations that had already been done in English, Latin, and French ─ and of course he had dictionaries. Translators always draw on more than one source text. Even when the scene of translation consists of just one person with a pen, paper, and the book that is being translated, or even when it is just one person translating orally for another, that person's linguistic knowledge arises from lots of other texts and other conversations. And then his or her idea of the translation's purpose will be influenced by the expectations of the person or people it is for. In both these senses every translation is a crowd translation.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸 (A), (B)에 들어갈 연결어로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: (A) '~일 때조차도'(양보) Even when, (B) 두 번째 근거 추가(연속) Moreover.
items.lead: Translators always draw on more than one source text. (A)＿＿＿ the scene of translation consists of just one person with a pen and paper, that person's linguistic knowledge arises from lots of other texts. (B)＿＿＿, his or her idea of the translation's purpose will be influenced by the expectations of the readers.

items.passage: A new piece of information that has to do with us will be more easily and thoroughly processed.

items.explain: thoroughly = '철저하게·완전히' = completely, fully. ② superficially(피상적으로)는 반의어 함정.

items.explain: '좋은 결과를 바라며 위험을 감수한 선택' = bet. ② venture는 의미가 가까운 함정이나 정의문은 bet에 더 가깝다.

items.passage: We might forget an anecdote about a stranger because it makes few connections with our existing associations, but we won't forget a piece of gossip about our cousin. There's one complex network that is larger and quicker to access than all others ─ the self. We've been thinking about ourselves in our whole lives. So if a new piece of information has something to do with us, it will be more easily and thoroughly processed. It hits even closer to home than our actual home ─ we can take a vacation away from our home, but not from ourselves. The most effective communicators find ways to make the abstract personal. Consider the warning that law schools give to motivate first-year law students concerning the rigors of their program. Hearing that "the first-year dropout rate is 33%" is an abstract statistic. "Look to your left, look to your right. One of the three of you won't be joining us next fall" wakes up the self.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 가장 크고 빠른 네트워크 = 자아(the self). 지문 전체의 핵심 개념.
items.lead: There is one complex network that is larger and quicker to access than all others — ＿＿＿＿.

items.passage: If we've invested in something that hasn't repaid us ─ be it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship ─ we find it very difficult to walk away. This is the sunk cost fallacy. Our instinct is to continue investing money or time as we hope that our investment will prove to be worthwhile in the end. Giving up would mean acknowledging that we've wasted something we can't get back, and that thought is so painful that we prefer to avoid it if we can. The problem, of course, is that if something really is a bad bet, then staying with it simply increases the amount we lose. Rather than walk away from a bad five-year relationship, for example, we turn it into a bad 10-year relationship; rather than accept that we've lost a thousand dollars, we lay down another thousand and lose that too. In the end, by delaying the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 보답하지 않는 투자를 고수하는 성향 = 매몰 비용 오류. 나머지는 다른 지문 핵심어로 구성된 크로스 함정.
items.lead: The tendency to stay with an investment that hasn't repaid us is known as the ＿＿＿＿.

items.passage: All translators feel some pressure from the community of readers for whom they are doing their work. And all translators arrive at their interpretations in dialogue with other people. The English poet Alexander Pope had pretty good Greek, but when he set about translating Homer's Iliad in the early 18th century he was not on his own. He had Greek commentaries to refer to, and translations that had already been done in English, Latin, and French ─ and of course he had dictionaries. Translators always draw on more than one source text. Even when the scene of translation consists of just one person with a pen, paper, and the book that is being translated, or even when it is just one person translating orally for another, that person's linguistic knowledge arises from lots of other texts and other conversations. And then his or her idea of the translation's purpose will be influenced by the expectations of the person or people it is for. In both these senses every translation is a crowd translation.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 두 가지 의미에서 모든 번역은 군중 번역(crowd translation). ② solitary는 정반대.
items.lead: In both these senses every translation is a ＿＿＿＿ translation.

items.passage: The explosion of popular music in the second half of the twentieth century as well as the global circulation and dissemination of music by the creative industries propelled a new understanding of accessibility in relation to music. Suddenly, in the 1950s, anyone could pick up spoons, a couple of pans, a second-hand guitar and start a band. This led to specific genres such as skiffle, but also, more generally, reflected a much more relaxed and inclusive attitude to music making. While ordinary people had always sung and made music, the popular music movement was driven by a spirit of rebellion and freedom. This approach led to the punk movement, whose musicians even made it a condition for their music to be non-virtuosic and accessible to all in the 1970s. Groups who had been entirely excluded from music revelled in opportunities to create. This led to a sense of novelty and empowerment in and beyond the music sphere.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 접근성 확대 → 참신함과 자율성(novelty and empowerment). 나머지 짝은 본문과 반대되거나 무관.
items.lead: This led to a sense of ＿＿＿＿ in and beyond the music sphere.

items.passage: 〈주어진 문장〉 Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. ── In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness, with the agreement that afterward they could keep one as a reward. ( ① ) Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. ( ② ) The poster that was no longer available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. ( ③ ) In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance. ( ④ ) When we are deprived of an option, we suddenly deem it more attractive. ( ⑤ )
items.stem: 글의 흐름으로 볼 때, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳은?
items.explain: '5분 후 통보' 문장은 포스터 배열 직후·재평가 이전이므로 ①에 들어간다.

items.passage: 〈주어진 문장〉 Translators always draw on more than one source text. ── The English poet Alexander Pope had pretty good Greek, but when he set about translating Homer's Iliad he was not on his own. ( ① ) He had Greek commentaries to refer to, and translations that had already been done in English, Latin, and French. ( ② ) Even when the scene of translation consists of just one person with a pen and paper, that person's linguistic knowledge arises from lots of other texts. ( ③ ) And then his or her idea of the translation's purpose will be influenced by the expectations of the readers. ( ④ ) In both these senses, every translation is a crowd translation. ( ⑤ )

items.explain: 사례 도입(B) → 추상 통계(A) → 자아를 깨우는 말(C) 순.

(B) Suddenly, in the 1950s, anyone could pick up everyday objects and a second-hand guitar and start a band. (C) This approach later led to the punk movement of the 1970s, open to all.

items.passage: (A)(What / That) Jobs understood was that a physical office was fundamentally similar to a virtual office. He knew that using familiar (B)terms would make the new technology easier to understand.

items.explain: (A) understood의 목적어 자리가 비어 있으므로 선행사를 포함한 관계대명사 What. (B) term = '용어'(words/expressions) — 학기·조건은 함정.

items.stem: 자아와 기억(로스쿨 경고)에 관한 진술 중 올바른(True) 것은?
options: "One of the three of you won't be joining us next fall" is meant to wake up the self.
"The dropout rate is 33%" is described as a personal, vivid message.
Law schools give the warning to discourage students from studying.
The abstract statistic is said to be more effective than the personal message.
The self is described as the smallest and slowest network of all.
items.explain: '셋 중 한 명은 내년에 없다'는 말이 자아를 깨운다(참). ② 통계는 추상적, ③ 동기 부여가 목적, ④ 개인화 메시지가 더 효과적, ⑤ 자아는 가장 크고 빠른 네트워크 → 모두 거짓.

items.passage: We collect stamps, coins, vintage cars even when they serve no practical purpose. The post office doesn't accept the old stamps, the banks don't take old coins, and the vintage cars are no longer allowed on the road. These are all side issues; the attraction is that they are in short supply. In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness ─ with the agreement that afterward they could keep one poster as a reward for their participation. Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. The poster that was no longer available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance: when we are deprived of an option, we suddenly deem it more attractive.
items.stem: 포스터 실험에 관한 진술 중 올바른(True) 것은?
options: It was the third-ranked poster, not the top one, that was made unavailable.
Students re-ranked only the nine remaining posters.
The removed poster was judged the least attractive afterward.
Students received ten posters as a reward for participating.
The study disproved the idea of reactance.
items.explain: 제거된 건 1위가 아니라 3위 포스터(참). ② 10장 전부 재평가, ③ 제거된 포스터가 가장 아름다움, ④ 보상은 1장, ⑤ 실험은 리액턴스를 입증 → 모두 거짓.

While ordinary people had always sung and made music, the popular music movement was driven by a spirit of rebellion and freedom. This approach led to the punk movement, whose musicians even made it a condition for their music to be non-virtuosic and accessible to all in the 1970s. items.stem: 다음 빈칸 (A), (B)에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: not only ~ but also의 변형(led to ~ but also … reflected) + more generally(더 일반적으로).
items.lead: This led to specific genres such as skiffle, (A)＿＿＿, (B)＿＿＿, reflected a much more relaxed and inclusive attitude to music making.

items.passage: Steve Jobs used analogy to get people to embrace the new technology. Before computers, people worked in a physical world. We used paper and pens and physical file folders and so on. The idea of working in a virtual world was radically different. Or at least seemed radically different. What Jobs understood was that a physical office was fundamentally similar to a virtual office. To win over the masses, Jobs drew strong analogies between the traditional workplace people knew well with the new, unfamiliar virtual workplace. In the pre-computer workplace, when ideas were written on paper it was called ... a document. When those documents needed to be stored they were put in ... a folder. And those folders were kept on ... a desk. Documents, folders, and desktops are the terms we use in our virtual work because Steve Jobs understood that using familiar terms would make the new technology easier to understand. The parallels between the physical and virtual workplace now seem obvious.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 seemed가 함의하는 바로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: In reality, the virtual and physical worlds were not so different after all.
The virtual world was completely impossible to understand.
People never accepted the idea of a virtual world.
The physical world was actually superior to the virtual one.
Jobs failed to notice any similarity between the two worlds.
items.explain: was → seemed로 정정한 것은 '실제로는 그리 다르지 않았다'는 반전 복선 → ①.
items.lead: The idea of working in a virtual world was radically different. Or at least, it seemed radically different.

items.lead: Steve Jobs used analogy to get people to embrace the new technology. ① Before computers, people worked in a physical world using paper, pens, and file folders. ② What Jobs understood was that a physical office was fundamentally similar to a virtual office. ③ Modern smartphones now contain more computing power than the spacecraft of the early space age. ④ So he drew strong analogies between the familiar workplace and the new virtual one. ⑤ Documents, folders, and desktops are the terms we use because of this insight.

items.stem: 다음 글의 밑줄 친 부분 (a)~(e) 중 어법상 틀린 것은?
items.explain: 주어 The familiar terms는 복수 → (d)is가 아니라 are drawn. (a)that(보어절)·(c)that(관계사)는 적절.

items.passage: If we've invested in something that hasn't repaid us ─ be it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship ─ we find it very difficult to walk away. This is the sunk cost fallacy. Our instinct is to continue investing money or time as we hope that our investment will prove to be worthwhile in the end. Giving up would mean acknowledging that we've wasted something we can't get back, and that thought is so painful that we prefer to avoid it if we can. The problem, of course, is that if something really is a bad bet, then staying with it simply increases the amount we lose. Rather than walk away from a bad five-year relationship, for example, we turn it into a bad 10-year relationship; rather than accept that we've lost a thousand dollars, we lay down another thousand and lose that too. In the end, by delaying the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 부분이 의미하는 바로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Postponing the decision makes our eventual loss even greater.
Admitting the problem solves it immediately.
The pain disappears if we simply wait long enough.
Investing more always reduces our suffering.
Time heals every bad investment in the end.
items.explain: 결정을 미루면 결국 손실이 더 커진다는 뜻. 나머지는 본문과 반대.
items.lead: In the end, by delaying the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it.

items.passage: The explosion of popular music in the second half of the twentieth century as well as the global circulation and dissemination of music by the creative industries propelled a new understanding of accessibility in relation to music. Suddenly, in the 1950s, anyone could pick up spoons, a couple of pans, a second-hand guitar and start a band. This led to specific genres such as skiffle, but also, more generally, reflected a much more relaxed and inclusive attitude to music making. While ordinary people had always sung and made music, the popular music movement was driven by a spirit of rebellion and freedom. This approach led to the punk movement, whose musicians even made it a condition for their music to be non-virtuosic and accessible to all in the 1970s. Groups who had been entirely excluded from music revelled in opportunities to create. This led to a sense of novelty and empowerment in and beyond the music sphere.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 부분이 의미하는 바로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Music making became open and welcoming to all kinds of people.
Musicians stopped caring about the quality of their music.
Only trained professionals were allowed to perform.
Music became more commercial and competitive.
items.explain: inclusive = '포용적·모두에게 열린'. ② 품질을 등한시했다는 과장 함정.
items.lead: The popular music movement reflected a much more relaxed and inclusive attitude to music making.

items.passage: When we receive an unfavorable outcome through an unfair process, we can reassure ourselves. (A) If the process is fair, however, we cannot externalize the outcome so easily. (B) We believe the bad outcome had little to do with us and everything to do with the unfairness. (C) This is the hidden cost of a fair process.
items.stem: 주어진 글 다음에 이어질 글의 순서로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 외부 귀인으로 안심(B) → 공정하면 그럴 수 없음(A) → 그것이 숨겨진 비용(C).

items.passage: 〈주어진 문장〉 But this was about more than just new genres. ── Suddenly anyone could pick up everyday objects and start a band. ( ① ) This led to specific genres such as skiffle. ( ② ) It reflected a much more relaxed and inclusive attitude to music making. ( ③ ) Groups previously excluded from music revelled in new opportunities to create. ( ④ ) This led to a sense of novelty and empowerment. ( ⑤ )
items.stem: 글의 흐름으로 볼 때, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳은?
items.explain: 'But this was about more than just new genres'는 'skiffle 같은 장르' 뒤, '태도' 설명 앞인 ②에 들어간다.

items.passage: We collect stamps, coins, vintage cars even when they serve no practical purpose. The post office doesn't accept the old stamps, the banks don't take old coins, and the vintage cars are no longer allowed on the road. These are all side issues; the attraction is that they are in short supply. In one study, students were asked to arrange ten posters in order of attractiveness ─ with the agreement that afterward they could keep one poster as a reward for their participation. Five minutes later, they were told that the poster with the third highest rating was no longer available. Then they were asked to judge all ten from scratch. The poster that was no longer available was suddenly classified as the most beautiful. In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance: when we are deprived of an option, we suddenly deem it more attractive.
items.stem: 다음 글에서 전체 흐름과 관계 없는 문장은?
items.explain: 우표·동전 수집 취미의 비용(④)은 리액턴스라는 흐름과 무관한 문장이다.
items.lead: People are strongly attracted to things that are scarce. ① In one study, students ranked ten posters and one was made unavailable. ② The unavailable poster was suddenly rated the most beautiful. ③ In psychology, this phenomenon is called reactance. ④ Collecting stamps and coins can be an expensive but rewarding lifelong hobby. ⑤ When our freedom to choose is removed, we want the thing all the more.

items.passage: The explosion of popular music in the second half of the twentieth century as well as the global circulation and dissemination of music by the creative industries propelled a new understanding of accessibility in relation to music. Suddenly, in the 1950s, anyone could pick up spoons, a couple of pans, a second-hand guitar and start a band. This led to specific genres such as skiffle, but also, more generally, reflected a much more relaxed and inclusive attitude to music making. While ordinary people had always sung and made music, the popular music movement was driven by a spirit of rebellion and freedom. This approach led to the punk movement, whose musicians even made it a condition for their music to be non-virtuosic and accessible to all in the 1970s. Groups who had been entirely excluded from music revelled in opportunities to create. This led to a sense of novelty and empowerment in and beyond the music sphere.
items.stem: 다음 두 글(리액턴스, 매몰 비용 오류)이 공통적으로 시사하는 바로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Human decisions are often driven by irrational psychological biases rather than objective value.
People always weigh costs and benefits in a fully rational way.
Scarcity and past investment have no effect on our judgment.
Emotional attachment generally improves financial decisions.
We tend to undervalue things that are hard to obtain.
items.explain: 두 글 모두 객관적 가치보다 비합리한 심리적 편향이 결정을 이끈다는 점을 공유한다.

items.passage: Steve Jobs used analogy to get people to embrace the new technology. Before computers, people worked in a physical world. We used paper and pens and physical file folders and so on. The idea of working in a virtual world was radically different. Or at least seemed radically different. What Jobs understood was that a physical office was fundamentally similar to a virtual office. To win over the masses, Jobs drew strong analogies between the traditional workplace people knew well with the new, unfamiliar virtual workplace. In the pre-computer workplace, when ideas were written on paper it was called ... a document. When those documents needed to be stored they were put in ... a folder. And those folders were kept on ... a desk. Documents, folders, and desktops are the terms we use in our virtual work because Steve Jobs understood that using familiar terms would make the new technology easier to understand. The parallels between the physical and virtual workplace now seem obvious.
items.stem: 다음 두 글(자아와 기억, Steve Jobs의 유추)이 공통적으로 강조하는 바는?
options: Connecting new information to what is already familiar or personal makes it easier to accept and understand.
Abstract statistics are the most persuasive form of communication.
New technology should be explained only with entirely new vocabulary.
People remember strangers better than they remember themselves.
Analogy tends to confuse rather than clarify new ideas.
items.explain: 새 정보를 친숙·개인적인 것과 연결할 때 수용·이해가 쉬워진다는 공통 메시지.

items.passage: All translators feel some pressure from the community of readers for whom they are doing their work. And all translators arrive at their interpretations in dialogue with other people. The English poet Alexander Pope had pretty good Greek, but when he set about translating Homer's Iliad in the early 18th century he was not on his own. He had Greek commentaries to refer to, and translations that had already been done in English, Latin, and French ─ and of course he had dictionaries. Translators always draw on more than one source text. Even when the scene of translation consists of just one person with a pen, paper, and the book that is being translated, or even when it is just one person translating orally for another, that person's linguistic knowledge arises from lots of other texts and other conversations. And then his or her idea of the translation's purpose will be influenced by the expectations of the person or people it is for. In both these senses every translation is a crowd translation.
items.stem: 다음 두 글(대중음악의 민주화, 번역의 집단성)이 공통적으로 부각하는 바는?
options: What looks like an individual act is in fact shaped by a wider community.
Professional expertise is the only path to good art.
Creativity declines when more people participate.
Tradition must be preserved against any change.
Solitary effort always produces the best results.
items.explain: 개인의 산물처럼 보이는 것(음악·번역)도 실은 더 넓은 공동체가 빚어낸다.

items.passage: We might forget an anecdote about a stranger because it makes few connections with our existing associations, but we won't forget a piece of gossip about our cousin. There's one complex network that is larger and quicker to access than all others ─ the self. We've been thinking about ourselves in our whole lives. So if a new piece of information has something to do with us, it will be more easily and thoroughly processed. It hits even closer to home than our actual home ─ we can take a vacation away from our home, but not from ourselves. The most effective communicators find ways to make the abstract personal. Consider the warning that law schools give to motivate first-year law students concerning the rigors of their program. Hearing that "the first-year dropout rate is 33%" is an abstract statistic. "Look to your left, look to your right. One of the three of you won't be joining us next fall" wakes up the self.
items.stem: 다음은 자아와 기억에 관한 글의 요약문이다. 빈칸 (A), (B)에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 추상을 개인화하는(personal) 쪽이 추상적인(abstract) 쪽보다 효과적이다.
items.lead: Because the self is the most accessible network, communicators who make abstract information (A)＿＿＿ rather than (B)＿＿＿ are far more effective.

items.passage: When we receive an unfavorable outcome, in some ways the very last thing we want to hear is that the process was fair. As outraging as the combination of an unfavorable outcome and an unfair process is, this combination also brings with it a consolation prize: the possibility of attributing the bad outcome to something other than ourselves. We may reassure ourselves by believing that our bad outcome had little to do with us and everything to do with the unfair process. If the process is fair, however, we cannot nearly as easily externalize the outcome; we got what we got "fair and square." When the process is fair we believe that our outcome is deserved, which is another way of saying that there must have been something about ourselves (what we did or who we are) that caused the outcome.
items.stem: 다음은 공정한 과정의 역설에 관한 글의 요약문이다. 빈칸 (A), (B)에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 공정하면 결과를 외부화(externalize)할 수 없어 위안(comfort)이 줄어든다.
items.lead: When a process is fair, we cannot (A)＿＿＿ a bad outcome, so a fair process can paradoxically bring less (B)＿＿＿ than an unfair one.

items.passage: If we've invested in something that hasn't repaid us ─ be it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship ─ we find it very difficult to walk away. This is the sunk cost fallacy. Our instinct is to continue investing money or time as we hope that our investment will prove to be worthwhile in the end. Giving up would mean acknowledging that we've wasted something we can't get back, and that thought is so painful that we prefer to avoid it if we can. The problem, of course, is that if something really is a bad bet, then staying with it simply increases the amount we lose. Rather than walk away from a bad five-year relationship, for example, we turn it into a bad 10-year relationship; rather than accept that we've lost a thousand dollars, we lay down another thousand and lose that too. In the end, by delaying the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.
items.stem: 매몰 비용 오류에 관한 글의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Never Give Up on Any Investment
How to Always Win Your Bets
items.explain: 때로는 손절하고 떠나라는 요지 → 'Knowing When to Walk Away'. ②④⑤는 동기부여처럼 들리지만 본문과 정반대인 함정.

is much easier to forget over time

items.explain: 개인적 메시지가 효과적인 이유는 가장 접근이 빠른 네트워크인 자아를 건드리기 때문.
items.lead: A vivid, personal message outperforms a dry statistic because it ＿＿＿＿.

items.explain: 선택의 자유가 '박탈될' 때 리액턴스가 생긴다(deprive와 연결).

items.passage: a set of written explanations or notes that help you understand a difficult text

items.explain: '어려운 텍스트 이해를 돕는 설명·주석' = commentary. 5번 지문 Pope 사례의 Greek commentaries.

items.passage: This is the hidden cost of a fair process.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 부분이 의미하는 바로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Fairness can deprive us of the comfort of blaming external causes.
A fair process is always financially expensive to run.
Fairness guarantees a better and happier outcome.
Unfair processes have no real downsides at all.
Hidden fees make fair processes secretly costly.

options: Refusing to quit a bad situation only prolongs and deepens it.
Long relationships are always better than short ones.
Time automatically fixes a bad relationship.
We should invest even more in failing relationships.
Five years is the ideal length for a relationship.

items.lead: The self is the brain's largest and most accessible network. ① Information related to us is processed more easily. ② Effective communicators find ways to make the abstract personal. ③ A vivid warning wakes up the self better than a statistic does. ④ The human brain weighs about 1.4 kilograms on average. ⑤ This is why personal framing is so persuasive.

items.passage: Investing in something that hasn't repaid us makes walking away hard. (A) The problem is that staying with a bad bet only increases our loss. (B) Our instinct is to keep investing, hoping it will prove worthwhile. (C) So sometimes we just have to cut our losses.
items.stem: 주어진 글 다음에 이어질 글의 순서로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 계속 투자 본능(B) → 하지만 손실만 늘어남(A) → 그래서 손절(C).

items.passage: 〈주어진 문장〉 Ironically, this comes at a price. ── A fair process feels right and just. ( ① ) When the process is fair, we cannot externalize a bad outcome. ( ② ) We are forced to believe the cause lay partly in ourselves. ( ③ ) This is the hidden cost of fairness. ( ④ ) So fairness, for all its virtues, can leave us less comforted. ( ⑤ )
items.stem: 글의 흐름으로 볼 때, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳은?
items.explain: 'Ironically, this comes at a price'는 '공정한 과정은 올바르게 느껴진다' 직후의 반전 신호이므로 ①에 들어간다.

items.passage:
When we receive an unfavorable outcome, in some ways the very last thing we want to hear is that the process was fair. As outraging as the combination of an unfavorable outcome and an unfair process is, this combination also brings with it a consolation prize: the possibility of attributing the bad outcome to something other than ourselves. We may reassure ourselves by believing that our bad outcome had little to do with us and everything to do with the unfair process. If the process is fair, however, we cannot nearly as easily externalize the outcome; we got what we got "fair and square." When the process is fair we believe that our outcome is deserved, which is another way of saying that there must have been something about ourselves (what we did or who we are) that caused the outcome.

If we've invested in something that hasn't repaid us ─ be it money in a failing venture, or time in an unhappy relationship ─ we find it very difficult to walk away. This is the sunk cost fallacy. Our instinct is to continue investing money or time as we hope that our investment will prove to be worthwhile in the end. Giving up would mean acknowledging that we've wasted something we can't get back, and that thought is so painful that we prefer to avoid it if we can. The problem, of course, is that if something really is a bad bet, then staying with it simply increases the amount we lose. Rather than walk away from a bad five-year relationship, for example, we turn it into a bad 10-year relationship; rather than accept that we've lost a thousand dollars, we lay down another thousand and lose that too. In the end, by delaying the pain of admitting our problem, we only add to it. Sometimes we just have to cut our losses.
items.stem: 다음 두 글(공정한 과정의 역설, 매몰 비용 오류)이 공통적으로 보여주는 인간 심리는?
options: We deal with loss in ways that are emotionally driven, not strictly rational.
We always make decisions that maximize our material gain.
We are completely indifferent to how outcomes are produced.
Fairness and investment never affect our feelings.
We easily let go of bad outcomes and investments.
items.explain: 두 글 모두 손실을 다루는 방식이 이성이 아니라 감정에 좌우됨을 보여준다.

items.passage: 〈가〉 희소한 것일수록 더 매력적으로 느껴진다. (13강·리액턴스)  〈나〉 금본위제는 화폐의 발행량을 제한해 그 가치를 뒷받침한다. (17강)
items.stem: 다음 두 개념을 종합할 때 가장 적절한 진술은? (Unit 크로스 13↔17강)
options: Limiting the supply of something can increase its perceived value.
Abundant resources are always more valuable than scarce ones.
Value depends only on an object's objective usefulness.
Psychological bias plays no role in economics.
Restricting supply lowers demand for a thing.
items.explain: 〈가〉 희소→매력, 〈나〉 발행량 제한→가치 유지. 공급을 제한하면 인지된 가치가 올라간다는 공통 원리. ⑤는 수요·공급 방향을 뒤집은 함정.

items.passage: 〈가〉 대중음악은 접근성을 높여 누구나 창작에 참여할 수 있게 했다. (13강)  〈나〉 양방향 인터넷은 소수의 제작자에게 국한되던 참여를 대중에게 개방했다. (17강)
items.stem: 다음 두 개념이 공통적으로 시사하는 바는? (Unit 크로스 13↔17강)
options: New tools can democratize participation that was once limited to a few.
Only experts ever benefit from new media.
Access to culture has not changed over time.
items.explain: 〈가〉 음악 접근성, 〈나〉 양방향 인터넷 모두 소수에게 국한되던 참여를 대중에게 개방한다(민주화).

items.passage: 〈가〉 신체는 체지방으로 에너지를 효율적으로 저장·관리한다. (19강)  〈나〉 인간은 매몰 비용에 집착해 자원을 비효율적으로 낭비한다. (13강)
items.stem: 다음 두 글의 대조가 시사하는 바로 가장 적절한 것은? (Unit 크로스 13↔19강)
options: Unlike the body's efficient energy management, human decision-making can cling irrationally to past investments.
The human body and mind both manage their resources perfectly.
Storing body fat is itself an example of the sunk cost fallacy.
Rational calculation governs all human behavior.
Energy and money always follow identical rules.
items.explain: 〈가〉 신체의 효율적 자원 관리 vs 〈나〉 마음의 비효율적 집착의 대조. ③ 체지방 저장을 매몰비용으로 본 것은 오독 함정.

items.passage: All translators feel some pressure from the community of readers for whom they are doing their work. And all translators arrive at their interpretations in dialogue with other people. The English poet Alexander Pope had pretty good Greek, but when he set about translating Homer's Iliad in the early 18th century he was not on his own. He had Greek commentaries to refer to, and translations that had already been done in English, Latin, and French ─ and of course he had dictionaries. Translators always draw on more than one source text. Even when the scene of translation consists of just one person with a pen, paper, and the book that is being translated, or even when it is just one person translating orally for another, that person's linguistic knowledge arises from lots of other texts and other conversations. And then his or her idea of the translation's purpose will be influenced by the expectations of the person or people it is for. In both these senses every translation is a crowd translation.
items.stem: 다음은 번역의 집단성에 관한 글의 요약문이다. 빈칸 (A), (B)에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 혼자의 일(solitary)처럼 보이지만 실은 집단적(collective) 행위.
items.lead: Although translation may look like a (A)＿＿＿ task, a translator's knowledge and aims are shaped by many other texts and readers, so every translation is really a (B)＿＿＿ one.

items.passage: Several of these passages suggest that human judgment is often shaped less by objective facts than by ＿＿＿＿.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 여러 지문(자아·리액턴스·매몰비용·공정 과정)이 공통으로 시사하는 바: 판단은 객관적 사실보다 심리적 프레이밍·편향에 좌우된다.

items.passage: All translators feel some pressure from the community of readers for whom they are doing their work. And all translators arrive at their interpretations in dialogue with other people. The English poet Alexander Pope had pretty good Greek, but when he set about translating Homer's Iliad in the early 18th century he was not on his own. He had Greek commentaries to refer to, and translations that had already been done in English, Latin, and French ─ and of course he had dictionaries. Translators always draw on more than one source text. Even when the scene of translation consists of just one person with a pen, paper, and the book that is being translated, or even when it is just one person translating orally for another, that person's linguistic knowledge arises from lots of other texts and other conversations. And then his or her idea of the translation's purpose will be influenced by the expectations of the person or people it is for. In both these senses every translation is a crowd translation.
items.stem: 번역의 집단성에 관한 글의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Every Translation Is a Crowd Translation
The Lonely Genius of the Translator
items.explain: 핵심 명제 '모든 번역은 군중 번역' → 'Every Translation Is a Crowd Translation'. ② 고독한 천재는 정반대 함정.

items.passage: According to Einstein's theory, a large mass like the Sun 'bends' space-time.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 단어와 의미가 가장 가까운 것은?
items.explain: bend = '휘게 하다' = curve. ① straighten(곧게 펴다)은 반의어 함정.

items.passage: The shift was much larger than Newton's theory predicted.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 단어와 의미가 가장 가까운 것은?
items.explain: predict = '예측하다' = forecast. ① observe(관측하다)는 내용상 헷갈리는 함정.

items.passage: a feeling or emotion, often one that influences how you act
items.stem: 다음 영영 풀이(English definition)에 해당하는 단어는?
items.explain: '행동에 영향을 주는 감정·정서' = sentiment. 2번 지문의 핵심 어휘.

items.passage: Stars aren't visible during the day when the Sun is out.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 단어의 반의어로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: visible(보이는)의 반의어 = invisible. 낮에는 별이 보이지 않는다는 내용.

items.passage: Minimal processing can be one of the best ways to keep original flavors.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 단어의 반의어로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: minimal(최소의)의 반의어 = maximal(최대의). ④ efficient는 본문에 나오지만 반의어가 아니다.

items.passage: Our legs and feet reacted instantaneously, without the need for conscious thought.
items.stem: 다음 밑줄 친 단어의 반의어로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: conscious(의식적인)의 반의어 = unconscious. 발·다리가 의식적 사고 없이 즉각 반응했다는 내용.

items.passage: According to Einstein's theory, a large mass like the Sun 'bends' space-time. Newton's theory makes no such prediction. This bending of space-time leads to phenomena such as 'gravitational lensing' where the light of distant stars appears to be in different locations when they pass by a large mass like the Sun. We don't normally see this lensing because stars aren't visible during the day when the Sun is out, but a solar eclipse in 1919 allowed scientists to observe what the Sun's gravity was doing to the light from distant stars. The stars around the Sun appeared to have moved from their normal positions in the night sky. The shift was much larger than Newton's theory predicted, but exactly in the positions predicted by Einstein's theory.
items.stem: 상대성 이론과 일식 관측에 관한 글의 내용과 일치하는 것은?
options: Newton's theory predicted that a large mass bends space-time.
In 1919, a solar eclipse let scientists observe the Sun's effect on starlight.
Stars are easily visible near the Sun during the daytime.
The observed shift was smaller than Newton had predicted.
The shift did not match Einstein's prediction at all.
items.explain: 1919년 일식이 태양의 빛 굴절 관측을 가능케 함(일치). ① 뉴턴은 그런 예측을 하지 않음, ③ 낮엔 별이 안 보임, ④ 이동 폭은 뉴턴 예측보다 큼, ⑤ 아인슈타인 예측과 정확히 일치.

items.passage: We are the only species that seasons its food, deliberately altering it with the highly flavored plant parts we call herbs and spices. It's quite possible that our taste for spices has an evolutionary root. Many spices have antibacterial properties ─ in fact, common seasonings such as garlic, onion, and oregano inhibit the growth of almost every bacterium tested. And the cultures that make the heaviest use of spices ─ think of the garlic and black pepper of Thai food, the ginger and coriander of India, the chili peppers of Mexico ─ come from warmer climates, where bacterial spoilage is a bigger issue. In contrast, the most lightly spiced cuisines ─ those of Scandinavia and northern Europe ─ are from cooler climates. Our uniquely human attention to flavor, in this case the flavor of spices, turns out to have arisen as a matter of life and death.
items.stem: 향신료와 생존에 관한 글의 내용과 일치하는 것은?
options: Spices generally promote the growth of bacteria.
Scandinavian cuisine uses the largest amount of spices.
Humans are one of many species that season their food.
Our taste for spices has nothing to do with survival.
Cultures in warmer climates tend to make the heaviest use of spices.
items.explain: 더운 기후 문화권이 향신료를 가장 많이 씀(일치). ① 향신료는 박테리아 성장을 억제, ② 스칸디나비아는 적게 씀, ③ 인간은 양념하는 유일한 종, ④ 미각은 생존과 관련.

items.passage: As the old joke goes: "Software, free. User manual, $10,000." But it's no joke. A couple of high-profile companies make their living selling instruction and paid support for free software. The copy of code, being mere bits, is free. The lines of free code become valuable to you only through support and guidance. A lot of medical and genetic information will go this route in the coming decades. Right now getting a full copy of all your DNA is very expensive ($10,000), but soon it won't be. The price is dropping so fast, it will be $100 soon, and then the next year insurance companies will offer to sequence you for free. When a copy of your sequence costs nothing, the interpretation of what it means, what you can do about it, and how to use it ─ the manual for your genes ─ will be expensive.
items.stem: 유전자 설명서에 관한 글의 내용과 일치하지 않는 것은?
options: A copy of code is free because it is merely bits.
Some companies make a living by selling support for free software.
The cost of getting a full copy of your DNA is falling fast.
In the future, interpreting your DNA will become cheaper than copying it.
Free code becomes valuable to you only through support and guidance.
items.explain: 본문은 사본이 공짜가 될수록 '해석(설명서)이 비싸진다'고 한다. ④ '해석이 복제보다 싸진다'는 정반대 → 불일치. 나머지는 본문 진술.

items.passage: According to Einstein's theory, a large mass like the Sun ＿＿＿＿ space-time.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 어법상 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 단수 주어 a large mass에 수일치하는 단수 동사 bends. like the Sun은 수식어.

items.passage: These age-old reactions are still ＿＿＿＿ hardwired in us that our feet and legs still react as they did in prehistoric times.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 어법상 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: so + 형용사(hardwired) + that절 '매우 ~해서 ~하다' 구문. ② such는 명사구를 받으므로 불가.

items.passage: Milling of cereals is one of the most harsh processes which ＿＿＿＿ nutrient content.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 어법상 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 관계사 which의 선행사는 processes(복수) → 복수동사 affect. ① affects는 선행사를 Milling(단수)으로 오인한 함정.

items.passage: The writer and zoologist Desmond Morris observed that our feet communicate exactly what we think and feel more honestly than any other part of our bodies. Why are the feet and legs such accurate reflectors of our sentiments? For millions of years, long before humans spoke, our legs and feet reacted to environmental threats (e.g., hot sand, ill-tempered lions) instantaneously, without the need for conscious thought. Our limbic brains made sure that our feet and legs reacted as needed by either ceasing motion, running away, or kicking at a potential threat. This survival regimen, retained from our ancestral heritage, has served us well and continues to do so today. In fact, these age-old reactions are still so hardwired in us that when we are presented with something dangerous or even disagreeable, our feet and legs still react as they did in prehistoric times.
items.stem: 발과 다리에 관한 글의 주제로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: how to train our legs to run faster
why our feet and legs honestly reveal our true feelings
the detailed anatomy of the human limbic brain
the reason humans first learned to speak
the dangers of hot sand and wild animals
items.explain: 발·다리가 감정을 가장 정직하게 드러낸다는 내용. 나머지는 세부 소재를 과장한 함정.

items.passage: Minimal processing can be one of the best ways to keep original flavors and taste, without any need to add artificial flavoring or additives, or too much salt. This would also be the efficient way to keep most nutrients, especially the most sensitive ones such as many vitamins and anti-oxidants. Milling of cereals is one of the most harsh processes which dramatically affect nutrient content. While grains are naturally very rich in micronutrients, anti-oxidants and fiber (i.e. in wholemeal flour or flakes), milling usually removes the vast majority of minerals, vitamins and fibers to raise white flour. Such a spoilage of key nutrients and fiber is no longer acceptable in the context of a sustainable diet aiming at an optimal nutrient density and health protection. In contrast, fermentation of various foodstuffs or germination of grains are traditional, locally accessible, low-energy and highly nutritious processes of sounded interest.
items.stem: 최소 가공에 관한 글의 요지로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Milling is the healthiest way to process grains.
Artificial additives clearly improve the taste of food.
White flour is more nutritious than wholemeal flour.
Minimal processing best preserves a food's natural flavor and nutrients.
Fermentation destroys most of the nutrients in food.
items.explain: 최소 가공이 본연의 맛과 영양소를 가장 잘 보존한다는 요지. 나머지는 본문과 반대이거나 본문이 부정하는 내용.

items.passage: According to Einstein's theory, a large mass like the Sun 'bends' space-time. Newton's theory makes no such prediction. This bending of space-time leads to phenomena such as 'gravitational lensing' where the light of distant stars appears to be in different locations when they pass by a large mass like the Sun. We don't normally see this lensing because stars aren't visible during the day when the Sun is out, but a solar eclipse in 1919 allowed scientists to observe what the Sun's gravity was doing to the light from distant stars. The stars around the Sun appeared to have moved from their normal positions in the night sky. The shift was much larger than Newton's theory predicted, but exactly in the positions predicted by Einstein's theory.
items.stem: 상대성 이론에 관한 글의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: How to Observe the Sun Safely
items.explain: 1919년 일식 관측이 아인슈타인 이론을 입증 → 'A Solar Eclipse Confirms Einstein'. ② 뉴턴의 승리는 정반대 함정.

items.passage: As the old joke goes: "Software, free. User manual, $10,000." But it's no joke. A couple of high-profile companies make their living selling instruction and paid support for free software. The copy of code, being mere bits, is free. The lines of free code become valuable to you only through support and guidance. A lot of medical and genetic information will go this route in the coming decades. Right now getting a full copy of all your DNA is very expensive ($10,000), but soon it won't be. The price is dropping so fast, it will be $100 soon, and then the next year insurance companies will offer to sequence you for free. When a copy of your sequence costs nothing, the interpretation of what it means, what you can do about it, and how to use it ─ the manual for your genes ─ will be expensive.
items.stem: 유전자 설명서에 관한 글의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Why DNA Copies Will Always Be Costly
The End of All Free Software
Not the Data but Its Interpretation Is Valuable
The Death of the User Manual
items.explain: 데이터(사본)가 아니라 그 해석이 가치 있다는 내용 → 'Not the Data but Its Interpretation Is Valuable'.

items.explain: the most sensitive ones의 ones는 앞의 nutrients를 받는 반복 대명사. vitamins·anti-oxidants는 그 nutrients의 예시일 뿐이다.
items.lead: This would also be the efficient way to keep most nutrients, especially the most sensitive ones such as many vitamins and anti-oxidants.

items.lead: The most lightly spiced cuisines ─ those of Scandinavia and northern Europe ─ are from cooler climates.
