items.explain: 영양소를 파괴하는 제분(용인 불가) ↔ 영양가 높은 발효·발아의 대조 → In contrast.

items.explain: 더운 기후(향신료 多) ↔ 서늘한 기후(향신료 少)의 대조 → In contrast. ② Likewise는 유사 관계로 오답.

items.stem: 밑줄 친 부분 중 문맥상 낱말의 쓰임이 적절하지 않은 것은?
items.explain: 일식은 태양 중력이 별빛에 하는 일을 '관찰(observe)'하게 해 준 것이므로 ⑤ignore(무시하다)는 부적절 → observe가 맞다.

The shift was much larger than Newton's theory predicted, but exactly in the positions predicted by Einstein's theory.

items.passage: As the old joke goes: "Software, free. User manual, $10,000." But it's no joke. A couple of high-profile companies make their living selling instruction and paid support for free software. The copy of code, being mere bits, is free. The lines of free code become valuable to you only through support and guidance. A lot of medical and genetic information will go this route in the coming decades. Right now getting a full copy of all your DNA is very expensive ($10,000), but soon it won't be. The price is dropping so fast, it will be $100 soon, and then the next year insurance companies will offer to sequence you for free. When a copy of your sequence costs nothing, the interpretation of what it means, what you can do about it, and how to use it ─ the manual for your genes ─ will be expensive.

items.lead: When a copy of your sequence costs nothing, the ＿＿＿＿ of what it means, what you can do about it, and how to use it ─ the manual for your genes ─ will be expensive.

items.passage: Minimal processing can be one of the best ways to keep original flavors and taste, without any need to add artificial flavoring or additives, or too much salt. This would also be the efficient way to keep most nutrients, especially the most sensitive ones such as many vitamins and anti-oxidants. Milling of cereals is one of the most harsh processes which dramatically affect nutrient content. While grains are naturally very rich in micronutrients, anti-oxidants and fiber (i.e. in wholemeal flour or flakes), milling usually removes the vast majority of minerals, vitamins and fibers to raise white flour. Such a spoilage of key nutrients and fiber is no longer acceptable in the context of a sustainable diet aiming at an optimal nutrient density and health protection. In contrast, fermentation of various foodstuffs or germination of grains are traditional, locally accessible, low-energy and highly nutritious processes of sounded interest.

items.lead: Milling removes the vast majority of minerals, vitamins and fibers to raise white flour. ＿(A)＿, such a spoilage of key nutrients is no longer acceptable for a sustainable diet. ＿(B)＿, fermentation and germination are traditional, low-energy and highly nutritious processes.

items.passage: According to Einstein's theory, a large mass like the Sun bends space-time, but Newton's theory makes no such prediction. (A) We don't normally see this lensing because stars aren't visible during the day when the Sun is out, but a solar eclipse in 1919 allowed scientists to observe what the Sun's gravity was doing to starlight. (B) This bending of space-time leads to a phenomenon called gravitational lensing, where the light of distant stars appears to be in different locations when it passes a large mass. (C) During the eclipse, the stars around the Sun appeared to have moved. The shift was much larger than Newton predicted, but exactly where Einstein's theory said it would be.
items.stem: 주어진 글 다음에 이어질 글의 순서로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 이론 제시 → (B) 현상(중력 렌즈) 정의 → (A) 평소엔 관측 불가하나 1919 일식이 기회 제공 → (C) 관측 결과(아인슈타인 입증).

( ② ) Many spices have antibacterial properties; common seasonings such as garlic and oregano inhibit the growth of almost every bacterium tested. ( ③ ) And the cultures that make the heaviest use of spices come from warmer climates, where bacterial spoilage is a bigger issue. ( ④ ) Our uniquely human attention to flavor turns out to have arisen as a matter of life and death. ( ⑤ )

items.passage: The writer and zoologist Desmond Morris observed that our feet communicate exactly what we think and feel more honestly than any other part of our bodies. Why are the feet and legs such accurate reflectors of our sentiments? For millions of years, long before humans spoke, our legs and feet reacted to environmental threats (e.g., hot sand, ill-tempered lions) instantaneously, without the need for conscious thought. Our limbic brains made sure that our feet and legs reacted as needed by either ceasing motion, running away, or kicking at a potential threat. This survival regimen, retained from our ancestral heritage, has served us well and continues to do so today. In fact, these age-old reactions are still so hardwired in us that when we are presented with something dangerous or even disagreeable, our feet and legs still react as they did in prehistoric times.
items.stem: 발과 다리에 관한 글의 내용과 일치하는 것은?
options: Feet communicate feelings less honestly than the face.
These survival reactions disappeared in modern humans.
Our limbic brains make sure our feet and legs react to threats.
Conscious thought is needed before our legs react.
Desmond Morris is best known as a famous chef.
items.explain: 변연계 뇌가 발·다리 반응을 보장(일치). ① 발이 얼굴보다 정직(반대), ② 현대에 사라짐(반대), ④ 의식적 사고 불필요(반대), ⑤ Morris는 동물학자·작가.

items.passage: As the old joke goes: "Software, free. User manual, $10,000." But it's no joke. A couple of high-profile companies make their living selling instruction and paid support for free software. The copy of code, being mere bits, is free. The lines of free code become valuable to you only through support and guidance. A lot of medical and genetic information will go this route in the coming decades. Right now getting a full copy of all your DNA is very expensive ($10,000), but soon it won't be. The price is dropping so fast, it will be $100 soon, and then the next year insurance companies will offer to sequence you for free. When a copy of your sequence costs nothing, the interpretation of what it means, what you can do about it, and how to use it ─ the manual for your genes ─ will be expensive.

options: how to copy your DNA at home for free
why interpretation, not raw data, will become the costly part
the history of well-known free software companies
why health insurance is becoming more expensive
the technical steps involved in DNA sequencing

items.passage: We are the only species that seasons its food, deliberately altering it with the highly flavored plant parts we call herbs and spices. It's quite possible that our taste for spices has an evolutionary root. Many spices have antibacterial properties ─ in fact, common seasonings such as garlic, onion, and oregano inhibit the growth of almost every bacterium tested. And the cultures that make the heaviest use of spices ─ think of the garlic and black pepper of Thai food, the ginger and coriander of India, the chili peppers of Mexico ─ come from warmer climates, where bacterial spoilage is a bigger issue. In contrast, the most lightly spiced cuisines ─ those of Scandinavia and northern Europe ─ are from cooler climates. Our uniquely human attention to flavor, in this case the flavor of spices, turns out to have arisen as a matter of life and death.
items.stem: 향신료에 관한 글의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Why Cold Climates Love Hot Peppers
Spices: A Tasty Tool for Survival
items.explain: 향신료의 항균성이 더운 기후 생존에 기여했다는 글 → 'Spices: A Tasty Tool for Survival'. ① 서늘한 기후가 향신료를 좋아한다는 것은 반대.

items.passage: According to Einstein's theory, a large mass like the Sun 'bends' space-time. Newton's theory makes no such prediction. This bending of space-time leads to phenomena such as 'gravitational lensing' where the light of distant stars appears to be in different locations when they pass by a large mass like the Sun. We don't normally see this lensing because stars aren't visible during the day when the Sun is out, but a solar eclipse in 1919 allowed scientists to observe what the Sun's gravity was doing to the light from distant stars. The stars around the Sun appeared to have moved from their normal positions in the night sky. The shift was much larger than Newton's theory predicted, but exactly in the positions predicted by Einstein's theory.
items.stem: 상대성 이론에 관한 글의 요지로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Newton's predictions proved more accurate than Einstein's.
The 1919 eclipse provided evidence supporting Einstein over Newton.
Gravitational lensing has never actually been observed.
Starlight always travels in perfectly straight lines.
Eclipses make stars permanently change their position.
items.explain: 1919년 일식 관측이 아인슈타인 이론을 뉴턴보다 지지하는 증거 제공. ① 뉴턴이 더 정확은 반대, ③④⑤는 불일치.

In contrast, the most lightly spiced cuisines ─ those of Scandinavia and northern Europe ─ are from cooler climates. Our uniquely human attention to flavor, in this case the flavor of spices, turns out to have arisen as a matter of life and death.

The stars around the Sun appeared to have moved from their normal positions in the night sky. The shift was much larger than Newton's theory predicted, but exactly in the positions predicted by Einstein's theory.
items.stem: 밑줄 친 matched exactly the positions Einstein had calculated가 함축하는 바로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: The observed data fit Einstein's theory rather than Newton's.
The stars returned to their original places after the eclipse.
Newton's equations were finally confirmed by the measurement.
The Sun's gravity had no measurable effect on the light.
The eclipse made any precise measurement impossible.
items.explain: 관측값이 뉴턴이 아닌 아인슈타인 이론에 들어맞았다는 함의. ③뉴턴 확증은 정반대.
items.lead: The shift was much larger than Newton predicted, and matched exactly the positions Einstein had calculated.

items.passage: The writer and zoologist Desmond Morris observed that our feet communicate exactly what we think and feel more honestly than any other part of our bodies. Why are the feet and legs such accurate reflectors of our sentiments? For millions of years, long before humans spoke, our legs and feet reacted to environmental threats (e.g., hot sand, ill-tempered lions) instantaneously, without the need for conscious thought. Our limbic brains made sure that our feet and legs reacted as needed by either ceasing motion, running away, or kicking at a potential threat. This survival regimen, retained from our ancestral heritage, has served us well and continues to do so today. In fact, these age-old reactions are still so hardwired in us that when we are presented with something dangerous or even disagreeable, our feet and legs still react as they did in prehistoric times.
items.stem: 〈가〉 [B]의 발·다리 위협 반응과 〈나〉 교과서 L5에서 유진이 우주 유영 중 끝없는 허공 앞에서 느끼는 공포의 공통점은? (Unit 크로스 15↔교과서 L5)
options: Both are deliberate choices made after careful planning.
Both are cultural customs taught by older generations.
Both disappear completely once a person gains experience.
Both are instinctive reactions to danger that arise before conscious thought.
Both are caused simply by a lack of proper training.
items.explain: 발·다리 반응과 우주 유영의 공포는 모두 의식적 사고 이전에 일어나는 본능적 위협 반응. ①②(계획·관습)는 반대.

items.passage: According to Einstein's theory, a large mass like the Sun 'bends' space-time. Newton's theory makes no such prediction. This bending of space-time leads to phenomena such as 'gravitational lensing' where the light of distant stars appears to be in different locations when they pass by a large mass like the Sun. We don't normally see this lensing because stars aren't visible during the day when the Sun is out, but a solar eclipse in 1919 allowed scientists to observe what the Sun's gravity was doing to the light from distant stars. The stars around the Sun appeared to have moved from their normal positions in the night sky. The shift was much larger than Newton's theory predicted, but exactly in the positions predicted by Einstein's theory.
items.stem: 〈가〉 [A]의 1919년 일식 관측과 〈나〉 교과서 L4의 능행차·축성 기록(의궤·환어행렬도)이 공유하는 핵심 가치는? (Unit 크로스 15↔교과서 L4)
options: Records and observations matter only to a few specialists.
Careful observation and detailed records can preserve and prove the truth for later generations.
Scientific data is always more reliable than historical records.
The past should be forgotten in favor of newer theories.
Visual beauty matters more than accurate recording.
items.explain: 일식 관측과 정조 능행차·축성 기록은 모두 정밀한 관측·기록이 진실을 보존하고 후대에 입증한다는 가치를 공유. ④과거 망각은 정반대.

items.passage: We are the only species that seasons its food, deliberately altering it with the highly flavored plant parts we call herbs and spices. It's quite possible that our taste for spices has an evolutionary root. Many spices have antibacterial properties ─ in fact, common seasonings such as garlic, onion, and oregano inhibit the growth of almost every bacterium tested. And the cultures that make the heaviest use of spices ─ think of the garlic and black pepper of Thai food, the ginger and coriander of India, the chili peppers of Mexico ─ come from warmer climates, where bacterial spoilage is a bigger issue. In contrast, the most lightly spiced cuisines ─ those of Scandinavia and northern Europe ─ are from cooler climates. Our uniquely human attention to flavor, in this case the flavor of spices, turns out to have arisen as a matter of life and death.
items.stem: 〈가〉 [E]의 향신료 선호(더운 기후의 부패 위험 대응)와 〈나〉 교과서 L5에서 유진이 무중력 환경에 적응하는 과정이 공유하는 핵심 개념은? (Unit 크로스 15↔교과서 L5)
options: a temporary discomfort with no lasting purpose
a purely aesthetic preference unrelated to survival
a fixed trait that can never change over time
a deliberate rejection of one's environment
an adaptation that helps living things survive in their environment
items.explain: 향신료 선호와 무중력 적응은 모두 환경에서 생명체의 생존을 돕는 적응. ②③(미적·고정)은 반대.

items.passage: Minimal processing can be one of the best ways to keep original flavors and taste, without any need to add artificial flavoring or additives, or too much salt. This would also be the efficient way to keep most nutrients, especially the most sensitive ones such as many vitamins and anti-oxidants. Milling of cereals is one of the most harsh processes which dramatically affect nutrient content. While grains are naturally very rich in micronutrients, anti-oxidants and fiber (i.e. in wholemeal flour or flakes), milling usually removes the vast majority of minerals, vitamins and fibers to raise white flour. Such a spoilage of key nutrients and fiber is no longer acceptable in the context of a sustainable diet aiming at an optimal nutrient density and health protection. In contrast, fermentation of various foodstuffs or germination of grains are traditional, locally accessible, low-energy and highly nutritious processes of sounded interest.
items.stem: [C]를 한 문장으로 요약할 때, 빈칸 (A), (B)에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: (A) strips away(대부분 제거) / (B) preserve(보존). ②protects/destroy는 정반대.
items.lead: While milling (A)＿＿＿ most of a grain's nutrients, gentler methods such as fermentation (B)＿＿＿ them with little energy.

items.passage: We are the only species that seasons its food, deliberately altering it with the highly flavored plant parts we call herbs and spices. It's quite possible that our taste for spices has an evolutionary root. Many spices have antibacterial properties ─ in fact, common seasonings such as garlic, onion, and oregano inhibit the growth of almost every bacterium tested. And the cultures that make the heaviest use of spices ─ think of the garlic and black pepper of Thai food, the ginger and coriander of India, the chili peppers of Mexico ─ come from warmer climates, where bacterial spoilage is a bigger issue. In contrast, the most lightly spiced cuisines ─ those of Scandinavia and northern Europe ─ are from cooler climates. Our uniquely human attention to flavor, in this case the flavor of spices, turns out to have arisen as a matter of life and death.
items.stem: [E]의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: The Global Journey of Exotic Spices
Why Cold Climates Produce Better Cooks
Spice: A Survival Tool Disguised as Flavor
The Health Risks of Eating Too Much Garlic
items.explain: 향신료가 맛으로 위장한 생존 도구라는 주지 → 'Spice: A Survival Tool Disguised as Flavor'. ①무역사·④건강위험은 지엽적.

items.passage: The writer and zoologist Desmond Morris observed that our feet communicate exactly what we think and feel more honestly than any other part of our bodies. Why are the feet and legs such accurate reflectors of our sentiments? For millions of years, long before humans spoke, our legs and feet reacted to environmental threats (e.g., hot sand, ill-tempered lions) instantaneously, without the need for conscious thought. Our limbic brains made sure that our feet and legs reacted as needed by either ceasing motion, running away, or kicking at a potential threat. This survival regimen, retained from our ancestral heritage, has served us well and continues to do so today. In fact, these age-old reactions are still so hardwired in us that when we are presented with something dangerous or even disagreeable, our feet and legs still react as they did in prehistoric times.
items.stem: 밑줄 친 such accurate reflectors of our emotions가 함축하는 바로 가장 적절한 것은?

items.lead: Why are the feet and legs such accurate reflectors of our emotions?

items.passage: According to Einstein's theory, a large mass like the Sun 'bends' space-time. Newton's theory makes no such prediction. This bending of space-time leads to phenomena such as 'gravitational lensing' where the light of distant stars appears to be in different locations when they pass by a large mass like the Sun. We don't normally see this lensing because stars aren't visible during the day when the Sun is out, but a solar eclipse in 1919 allowed scientists to observe what the Sun's gravity was doing to the light from distant stars. The stars around the Sun appeared to have moved from their normal positions in the night sky. The shift was much larger than Newton's theory predicted, but exactly in the positions predicted by Einstein's theory.
items.stem: [A]의 요지로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: Newton's theory remains the best available model of gravity.
Solar eclipses are too dangerous to observe safely.
The 1919 eclipse gave the first strong evidence for Einstein's relativity.
Light from distant stars never bends near the Sun.
Scientists in 1919 failed to measure the star shift.

items.passage: Faced with regional labor shortages, many economists argue that planned migration can serve as an effective (A) rather than a mere problem.

items.passage: The Internet differs from most of the mass media it replaces in an obvious and very important way: it works in two directions. We can send messages through the network as well as receive them, which has made the system all the more useful. The ability to exchange information online, to upload as well as download, has turned the Net into a thoroughfare for business and commerce. With a few clicks, people can search virtual catalogues, place orders, track shipments, and update information in corporate databases. But the Net doesn't just connect us with businesses; it connects us with one another. It's a personal broadcasting medium as well as a commercial one. Millions of people use it to distribute their own digital creations, in the form of blogs, videos, photos, songs, and podcasts, as well as to critique, edit, or otherwise modify the creations of others.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 인터넷은 정보가 both directions(양방향)로 흐른다. ②media·③messages는 주변 어휘 오답.
items.lead: Unlike older one-way media, the Internet lets information move freely in both (A).

items.passage: Though tiny in number, vagrant birds can subtly (A) the ecology of the regions they reach.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 미조(길 잃은 새)가 생태를 바꾼다 → alter. ③protect·⑤repeat는 반대·무관.

items.passage: Imagine that seven out of ten working Americans got fired tomorrow. What would they all do? It's hard to believe you'd have an economy at all if you gave pink slips to more than half the labor force. But that is what the industrial revolution did to the workforce of the early 19th century. Two hundred years ago, 70 percent of American workers lived on the farm. Today automation has eliminated all but 1 percent of their jobs, replacing them with machines. But the displaced workers did not sit idle. Instead, automation created hundreds of millions of jobs in entirely new fields. Those who once farmed were now manning the factories that manufactured farm equipment, cars, and other industrial products. Since then, wave upon wave of new occupations have arrived ─ appliance repair person, food chemist, photographer, web designer ─ each building on previous automation. Today, the vast majority of us are doing jobs that no farmer from the 1800s could have imagined.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 연결어로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 일자리 소멸 ↔ 새 일자리 창출 대조 → however. ①therefore·⑤in short는 순접·요약.
items.lead: Machines eliminated countless farm jobs; (A), automation eventually created many new occupations in fields no one had imagined.

items.passage: The word "migration" is almost always reported in the popular media and even in scientific literature as a problem or a crisis. For example, migrants are assumed to overcrowd cities, clog up labor markets, and increase poverty. The other questionable assumption is that most migration is involuntary ─ people fleeing natural or man-made disasters. The reality, however, is more complex, and many migrants are simply seeking greater economic opportunity. Of course migration can and does create social and economic problems. But migration can also be a solution for many preexisting problems. For example, out-migration generally redistributes workers from places of labor surplus to areas where there is greater demand or more opportunity. Migration is generally selective of persons who are younger, healthier, more flexible, and more willing to endure hardship in hopes of a better life relative to their prospects in their places of origin. Most research that examines long-term outcomes of migration, including remittances and intergenerational mobility, finds positive "long-term" effects on places of origin and destination.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 연결어로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 통념(위기) → 실상(경제적 기회) 반전 강조 → in fact. ④nevertheless도 매력적이나 실상 제시라 in fact가 적합.
items.lead: Migration is widely treated as a crisis; (A), most migrants are simply pursuing greater economic opportunity.

items.passage: According to Hobbes, man is not a being who can act morally in spite of his instinct to protect his existence in the state of nature. Hence, the only place where morality and moral liberty will begin to find an application begins in a place where a sovereign power, namely the state, emerges. Hobbes thus describes the state of nature as a circumstance in which man's life is "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish and short". It means when people live without a general power to control them all, they are indeed in a state of war. In other words, Hobbes, who accepted that human beings are not social and political beings in the state of nature, believes that without the power human beings in the state of nature are "antisocial and rational based on their selfishness". Moreover, since society is not a natural phenomenon and there is no natural force bringing people together, what will bring them together as a society is not mutual affection according to Hobbes. It is, rather, mutual fear of men's present and future that assembles them, since the cause of fear is a common drive among people in the state of nature.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: It is not A but B that... 강조구문. 홉스는 사회 결성의 동인을 mutual fear(상호 공포)로 본다. ①rational·④sovereign은 함정 어휘.
items.lead: According to Hobbes, it is not natural affection but (A) that drives people to assemble into a commonwealth.

( ① ) Radio and television followed the same one-way pattern. ( ② ) The Internet, however, was built differently. ( ③ ) Users could now send information back as easily as they received it. ( ④ ) Businesses, broadcasters, and ordinary individuals alike became publishers. ( ⑤ )

items.passage: 〈주어진 문장〉 Yet entirely new fields of work soon emerged from the very technologies that had displaced them. ── When machines took over farm labor, millions of jobs vanished within a generation. ( ① ) Factories, offices, and service industries then expanded rapidly. ( ② ) These sectors demanded skills that had not existed before. ( ③ ) Workers retrained and moved into them. ( ④ ) Today most people owe their livelihoods to such shifts. ( ⑤ )

items.passage: Migration has long been framed as a burden on receiving societies. (A) These newcomers often fill labor gaps that locals cannot. (B) In reality, however, the picture is far more positive. (C) As a result, both migrants and host economies benefit.
items.stem: 주어진 글 다음에 이어질 순서로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 주제문(부담) → (B)실상 반전 → (A)부연(일자리 충원) → (C)결론(상호 이익). (B)-(A)-(C).

items.passage: Hobbes imagined a state of nature without any common authority. (A) Out of this dread, they agree to surrender power to a sovereign. (B) In such a condition, every person fears every other. (C) Thus the commonwealth is born from mutual fear, not love.
items.stem: 주어진 글 다음에 이어질 순서로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 자연상태 제시 → (B)만인의 공포 → (A)권력 양도 → (C)공포에서 탄생한 국가. (B)-(A)-(C).

items.passage: Sleep is clearly more than just resting. One curious fact is that animals that are hibernating also have periods of sleep. It comes as a surprise to most of us, but hibernation and sleep are not the same thing at all, at least not from a neurological and metabolic perspective. Hibernating is more like being anesthetized: the subject is unconscious but not actually asleep. So a hibernating animal needs to get a few hours of conventional sleep each day within the larger unconsciousness. A further surprise to most of us is that bears, the most famous of wintry sleepers, don't actually hibernate. Real hibernation involves profound unconsciousness and a dramatic fall in body temperature ─ often to around 32 degrees Fahrenheit. By this definition, bears don't hibernate, because their body temperature stays near normal and they are easily awakened. Their winter sleeps are more accurately called a state of torpor.
items.stem: 다음 글의 요약문 빈칸 (A), (B)에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 곰의 겨울잠은 체온이 약간만 떨어져 진정한 동면과 다름 → (A) different / (B) warm.
items.lead: A bear's winter sleep involves only a mild drop in body temperature and can be interrupted, unlike the deep, sustained shutdown of true hibernation. 〈요약〉 A bear's winter rest is (A)＿＿＿ from genuine hibernation, since its body stays comparatively (B)＿＿＿.

items.passage: Imagine that seven out of ten working Americans got fired tomorrow. What would they all do? It's hard to believe you'd have an economy at all if you gave pink slips to more than half the labor force. But that is what the industrial revolution did to the workforce of the early 19th century. Two hundred years ago, 70 percent of American workers lived on the farm. Today automation has eliminated all but 1 percent of their jobs, replacing them with machines. But the displaced workers did not sit idle. Instead, automation created hundreds of millions of jobs in entirely new fields. Those who once farmed were now manning the factories that manufactured farm equipment, cars, and other industrial products. Since then, wave upon wave of new occupations have arrived ─ appliance repair person, food chemist, photographer, web designer ─ each building on previous automation. Today, the vast majority of us are doing jobs that no farmer from the 1800s could have imagined.
items.stem: 글의 제목으로 가장 적절한 것은? (자동화 지문)
options: Why Farms Will Always Need Workers
The Permanent Threat of Mass Unemployment
Machines: The End of Human Labor
How Factories Replaced Every Office Job
Automation Destroys Old Jobs but Builds New Ones
items.explain: 자동화는 구직종을 없애는 동시에 새 직종을 창출 → ⑤. ②③④은 한쪽 면(소멸만/대체만)만 부각한 함정.

items.passage: The Internet differs from most of the mass media it replaces in an obvious and very important way: it works in two directions. We can send messages through the network as well as receive them, which has made the system all the more useful. The ability to exchange information online, to upload as well as download, has turned the Net into a thoroughfare for business and commerce. With a few clicks, people can search virtual catalogues, place orders, track shipments, and update information in corporate databases. But the Net doesn't just connect us with businesses; it connects us with one another. It's a personal broadcasting medium as well as a commercial one. Millions of people use it to distribute their own digital creations, in the form of blogs, videos, photos, songs, and podcasts, as well as to critique, edit, or otherwise modify the creations of others.
items.stem: 밑줄 친 부분의 의미로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: a road only experts may use
a route open to traffic in both directions
items.explain: thoroughfare는 사람·차량이 오가는 통로. two-way는 양방향 → '양쪽으로 트인 길'. ④one-way·②blocked는 반대 함정.
items.lead: The Internet has become a two-way thoroughfare for information.

items.passage: The big problem with money made by governments is that those who run the government are always faced with the temptation to make more of it and spend it. Whether among ancient kings or modern politicians, this has happened again and again over the centuries, leading to inflation and the many economic and social problems that follow from inflation. For this reason, many countries have preferred using gold, silver, or some other material that is inherently limited in supply, as money. It is a way of depriving governments of the power to expand the money supply to inflationary levels. Gold has long been considered ideal for this purpose, since the supply of gold in the world usually cannot be increased rapidly. When paper money is convertible into gold whenever the individual chooses to do so, then the money is said to be "backed up" by gold. This expression is misleading only if we imagine that the value of the gold is somehow transferred to the paper money, when in fact the real point is that the gold simply limits the amount of paper money that can be issued.
items.stem: 밑줄 친 부분의 의미로 가장 적절한 것은?
options: money whose value is guaranteed by gold reserves
money issued only by private banks
items.explain: be backed by gold = 금 보유로 가치가 보증됨. ①무제한 발행·⑤교환 불가는 정반대.
items.lead: For decades, paper money was backed by gold held in national vaults.

items.passage: The word "migration" is almost always reported in the popular media and even in scientific literature as a problem or a crisis. For example, migrants are assumed to overcrowd cities, clog up labor markets, and increase poverty. The other questionable assumption is that most migration is involuntary ─ people fleeing natural or man-made disasters. The reality, however, is more complex, and many migrants are simply seeking greater economic opportunity. Of course migration can and does create social and economic problems. But migration can also be a solution for many preexisting problems. For example, out-migration generally redistributes workers from places of labor surplus to areas where there is greater demand or more opportunity. Migration is generally selective of persons who are younger, healthier, more flexible, and more willing to endure hardship in hopes of a better life relative to their prospects in their places of origin. Most research that examines long-term outcomes of migration, including remittances and intergenerational mobility, finds positive "long-term" effects on places of origin and destination.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 이주는 일손이 남는(surplus) 지역에서 부족(need) 지역으로 이동. ①demand는 need와 중복되어 대조 구도가 깨짐.
items.lead: Migration shifts workers from areas of (A) to areas of need.

items.passage: According to Hobbes, man is not a being who can act morally in spite of his instinct to protect his existence in the state of nature. Hence, the only place where morality and moral liberty will begin to find an application begins in a place where a sovereign power, namely the state, emerges. Hobbes thus describes the state of nature as a circumstance in which man's life is "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish and short". It means when people live without a general power to control them all, they are indeed in a state of war. In other words, Hobbes, who accepted that human beings are not social and political beings in the state of nature, believes that without the power human beings in the state of nature are "antisocial and rational based on their selfishness". Moreover, since society is not a natural phenomenon and there is no natural force bringing people together, what will bring them together as a society is not mutual affection according to Hobbes. It is, rather, mutual fear of men's present and future that assembles them, since the cause of fear is a common drive among people in the state of nature.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 홉스의 자연상태는 만인의 공포(constant fear)가 지배. ②trust·④love는 정반대 함정.
items.lead: In Hobbes's state of nature, human life is ruled by (A).

items.passage: Sleep is clearly more than just resting. One curious fact is that animals that are hibernating also have periods of sleep. It comes as a surprise to most of us, but hibernation and sleep are not the same thing at all, at least not from a neurological and metabolic perspective. Hibernating is more like being anesthetized: the subject is unconscious but not actually asleep. So a hibernating animal needs to get a few hours of conventional sleep each day within the larger unconsciousness. A further surprise to most of us is that bears, the most famous of wintry sleepers, don't actually hibernate. Real hibernation involves profound unconsciousness and a dramatic fall in body temperature ─ often to around 32 degrees Fahrenheit. By this definition, bears don't hibernate, because their body temperature stays near normal and they are easily awakened. Their winter sleeps are more accurately called a state of torpor.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 곰의 겨울잠은 체온이 약간 떨어지고 깨울 수 있는 torpor. ①true hibernation·③ordinary sleep은 지문이 부정하는 개념.
items.lead: A bear's so-called winter sleep is more accurately described as (A).

items.passage: Imagine that seven out of ten working Americans got fired tomorrow. What would they all do? It's hard to believe you'd have an economy at all if you gave pink slips to more than half the labor force. But that is what the industrial revolution did to the workforce of the early 19th century. Two hundred years ago, 70 percent of American workers lived on the farm. Today automation has eliminated all but 1 percent of their jobs, replacing them with machines. But the displaced workers did not sit idle. Instead, automation created hundreds of millions of jobs in entirely new fields. Those who once farmed were now manning the factories that manufactured farm equipment, cars, and other industrial products. Since then, wave upon wave of new occupations have arrived ─ appliance repair person, food chemist, photographer, web designer ─ each building on previous automation. Today, the vast majority of us are doing jobs that no farmer from the 1800s could have imagined.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 양보(Although) 뒤에는 새 일자리 창출(creates)이 와야 논리가 맞다. ①eliminates는 앞 절과 중복.
items.lead: Although machines destroy old jobs, automation ultimately (A) entirely new fields of work.

items.passage: It is a common assumption that most vagrant birds are ultimately doomed, aside from the rare cases where individuals are able to reorientate and return to their normal ranges. In turn, it is also commonly assumed that vagrancy itself is a relatively unimportant biological phenomenon. This is undoubtedly true for the majority of cases, as the most likely outcome of any given vagrancy event is that the individual will fail to find enough resources, and/or be exposed to inhospitable environmental conditions, and perish. However, there are many lines of evidence to suggest that vagrancy can, on rare occasions, dramatically alter the fate of populations, species or even whole ecosystems. Despite being infrequent, these events can be extremely important when viewed at the timescales over which ecological and evolutionary processes unfold. The most profound consequences of vagrancy relate to the establishment of new breeding sites, new migration routes and wintering locations. Each of these can occur through different mechanisms, and at different frequencies, and they each have their own unique importance.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 미조(길 잃은 새)의 생태적(ecological) 중요성이 주제. ①economic·④political은 지문 밖 어휘.
items.lead: Despite their small numbers, vagrant birds carry surprising (A) importance.

items.stem: 다음 글에서 전체 흐름과 관계 없는 문장은?
items.explain: 이주의 순기능을 설명하는 흐름에서 '고대 무역로'(④)는 부자연스러운 이탈 문장이다.
items.lead: Migration is often portrayed as a burden, yet it frequently relieves labor shortages. ① Workers move from regions of surplus to regions of urgent need. ② Receiving economies gain skills and energy they lack. ③ Migrants, in turn, find better wages and opportunity. ④ Ancient trade routes were mostly used for spices and silk. ⑤ Seen this way, migration benefits both sides at once.

items.passage: The Internet differs from most of the mass media it replaces in an obvious and very important way: it works in two directions. We can send messages through the network as well as receive them, which has made the system all the more useful. The ability to exchange information online, to upload as well as download, has turned the Net into a thoroughfare for business and commerce. With a few clicks, people can search virtual catalogues, place orders, track shipments, and update information in corporate databases. But the Net doesn't just connect us with businesses; it connects us with one another. It's a personal broadcasting medium as well as a commercial one. Millions of people use it to distribute their own digital creations, in the form of blogs, videos, photos, songs, and podcasts, as well as to critique, edit, or otherwise modify the creations of others.
items.stem: 다음 글에서 전체 흐름과 관계 없는 문장은?
items.explain: 인터넷의 양방향성 흐름에서 '인쇄기 기원'(⑤)은 무관한 삽입 문장이다.
items.lead: Older media broadcast one message to many passive receivers. ① The Internet, by contrast, lets users send information back. ② This two-way flow turns audiences into publishers. ③ Anyone can now upload as easily as download. ④ Such interaction reshapes how we communicate. ⑤ Printing presses were first developed in fifteenth-century Europe.

items.passage: The number of migrants who cross national borders each year ①have ②risen sharply, a trend ③that economists ④say ⑤reflects global inequality.
items.stem: 밑줄 친 부분 중 어법상 틀린 것은?
items.explain: 주어는 The number of …(단수) → ①have는 has로. cf. A number of는 복수.

items.passage: It ①is mutual fear ②what assembles people ③into a commonwealth ④that ⑤protects them.
items.stem: 밑줄 친 부분 중 어법상 틀린 것은?
items.explain: It is X that ... 강조구문 → ②what은 that으로. 다른 밑줄은 정상.

items.passage: The gold standard ①guaranteed monetary ②stability but ③transferred the money supply, a constraint that ④reassured cautious ⑤investors.
items.stem: 밑줄 친 부분 중 문맥상 낱말의 쓰임이 적절하지 않은 것은?
items.explain: 금본위는 통화량을 제한하므로 ③transferred(이전했다)는 부적절 → limited/restricted가 적절.

items.passage: Migration eases a labor ①shortage by sending workers from areas of ②surplus to areas of ③need, thereby ④worsening the regional ⑤imbalance.

items.explain: 이주는 불균형을 완화하므로 ④worsening(악화)은 부적절 → easing이 적절.

items.passage: a means or channel by which something is communicated or expressed
items.stem: 다음 영영 풀이(English definition)에 해당하는 단어는?

items.stem: 다음 자료의 내용과 일치하는 것은?
options: Farm employment fell dramatically while overall jobs increased.
Most Americans still work on farms today.
The farm workforce stayed roughly constant.
Fewer than 1% worked on farms in the 1800s.
items.explain: 70%→1%로 농업 고용은 급락했지만 전체 고용은 증가(일치). ②④⑤는 수치 오독, ③은 본문과 반대.
items.lead: 〈자료〉 In the early 1800s, about 70% of American workers were farmers. Today, fewer than 1% are, yet total employment has grown enormously as new industries emerged.

items.passage: The big problem with money made by governments is that those who run the government are always faced with the temptation to make more of it and spend it. Whether among ancient kings or modern politicians, this has happened again and again over the centuries, leading to inflation and the many economic and social problems that follow from inflation. For this reason, many countries have preferred using gold, silver, or some other material that is inherently limited in supply, as money. It is a way of depriving governments of the power to expand the money supply to inflationary levels. Gold has long been considered ideal for this purpose, since the supply of gold in the world usually cannot be increased rapidly. When paper money is convertible into gold whenever the individual chooses to do so, then the money is said to be "backed up" by gold. This expression is misleading only if we imagine that the value of the gold is somehow transferred to the paper money, when in fact the real point is that the gold simply limits the amount of paper money that can be issued.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: deprive A of B = A에게서 B를 빼앗다. 금본위는 화폐 발행 자유를 제한함.
items.lead: The gold standard could (A) governments of the freedom to print money at will.

items.passage: Imagine that seven out of ten working Americans got fired tomorrow. What would they all do? It's hard to believe you'd have an economy at all if you gave pink slips to more than half the labor force. But that is what the industrial revolution did to the workforce of the early 19th century. Two hundred years ago, 70 percent of American workers lived on the farm. Today automation has eliminated all but 1 percent of their jobs, replacing them with machines. But the displaced workers did not sit idle. Instead, automation created hundreds of millions of jobs in entirely new fields. Those who once farmed were now manning the factories that manufactured farm equipment, cars, and other industrial products. Since then, wave upon wave of new occupations have arrived ─ appliance repair person, food chemist, photographer, web designer ─ each building on previous automation. Today, the vast majority of us are doing jobs that no farmer from the 1800s could have imagined.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 가장 적절한 것은? (all but)
items.explain: all but = 거의. '거의 없애지만 새 일자리를 계속 만든다'는 대조 → eliminated. ①created는 yet 이하와 중복.
items.lead: Automation has all but (A) traditional farm jobs, yet it keeps generating new ones.

items.passage: ①Had automation never ②occurred, the rural economy ③would have ④collapse under ⑤surplus labor.
items.stem: 밑줄 친 부분 중 어법상 틀린 것은? (가정법 도치)
items.explain: 가정법 도치(Had ... occurred)의 주절은 would have p.p. → ④collapse는 collapsed로.

items.passage: According to Hobbes, man is not a being who can act morally in spite of his instinct to protect his existence in the state of nature. Hence, the only place where morality and moral liberty will begin to find an application begins in a place where a sovereign power, namely the state, emerges. Hobbes thus describes the state of nature as a circumstance in which man's life is "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish and short". It means when people live without a general power to control them all, they are indeed in a state of war. In other words, Hobbes, who accepted that human beings are not social and political beings in the state of nature, believes that without the power human beings in the state of nature are "antisocial and rational based on their selfishness". Moreover, since society is not a natural phenomenon and there is no natural force bringing people together, what will bring them together as a society is not mutual affection according to Hobbes. It is, rather, mutual fear of men's present and future that assembles them, since the cause of fear is a common drive among people in the state of nature.
items.stem: 글의 흐름과 관계 없는 문장은?
items.explain: 홉스의 공포=사회 결합 논지에서 '현대 국가의 연례 선거'(⑤)는 무관 문장이다.
items.lead: Hobbes argued that fear, not affection, binds society. ① Without authority, all live in mutual dread. ② This dread drives them to surrender power. ③ A sovereign then secures peace. ④ So the commonwealth is born of fear. ⑤ Many modern nations hold yearly elections.

items.passage: It is a common assumption that most vagrant birds are ultimately doomed, aside from the rare cases where individuals are able to reorientate and return to their normal ranges. In turn, it is also commonly assumed that vagrancy itself is a relatively unimportant biological phenomenon. This is undoubtedly true for the majority of cases, as the most likely outcome of any given vagrancy event is that the individual will fail to find enough resources, and/or be exposed to inhospitable environmental conditions, and perish. However, there are many lines of evidence to suggest that vagrancy can, on rare occasions, dramatically alter the fate of populations, species or even whole ecosystems. Despite being infrequent, these events can be extremely important when viewed at the timescales over which ecological and evolutionary processes unfold. The most profound consequences of vagrancy relate to the establishment of new breeding sites, new migration routes and wintering locations. Each of these can occur through different mechanisms, and at different frequencies, and they each have their own unique importance.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: profound ecological effect를 낳는 행위 → alter(변화시키다). ②③preserve·stabilize는 '변화' 논지와 상충.
items.lead: Vagrant birds can (A) the genetics of isolated populations, producing a profound ecological effect.

items.explain: 금본위는 통화량을 제한하므로 ②expanded(확대)는 모순 → limited가 적절.

items.explain: profound = 매우 깊고 강렬한. ①shallow·②mild는 반의어 함정.

items.passage: Imagine that seven out of ten working Americans got fired tomorrow. What would they all do? It's hard to believe you'd have an economy at all if you gave pink slips to more than half the labor force. But that is what the industrial revolution did to the workforce of the early 19th century. Two hundred years ago, 70 percent of American workers lived on the farm. Today automation has eliminated all but 1 percent of their jobs, replacing them with machines. But the displaced workers did not sit idle. Instead, automation created hundreds of millions of jobs in entirely new fields. Those who once farmed were now manning the factories that manufactured farm equipment, cars, and other industrial products. Since then, wave upon wave of new occupations have arrived ─ appliance repair person, food chemist, photographer, web designer ─ each building on previous automation. Today, the vast majority of us are doing jobs that no farmer from the 1800s could have imagined.
items.stem: 다음 자료의 내용과 일치하는 것은?
options: Most jobs in 2000 already existed in 1900.
Job categories stayed stable across the century.
Only 30% of 2000 jobs were newly created.
A majority of 2000 jobs were in newly created occupations.
items.explain: 2000년 일자리의 약 70%가 1900년에 없던 직종 → 새 직종이 다수(일치). ④'30%만 신생'은 수치 오독.
items.lead: 〈자료〉 Of the jobs Americans held in 2000, roughly 70% were in occupations that did not exist in 1900.

items.passage: Sleep is clearly more than just resting. One curious fact is that animals that are hibernating also have periods of sleep. It comes as a surprise to most of us, but hibernation and sleep are not the same thing at all, at least not from a neurological and metabolic perspective. Hibernating is more like being anesthetized: the subject is unconscious but not actually asleep. So a hibernating animal needs to get a few hours of conventional sleep each day within the larger unconsciousness. A further surprise to most of us is that bears, the most famous of wintry sleepers, don't actually hibernate. Real hibernation involves profound unconsciousness and a dramatic fall in body temperature ─ often to around 32 degrees Fahrenheit. By this definition, bears don't hibernate, because their body temperature stays near normal and they are easily awakened. Their winter sleeps are more accurately called a state of torpor.
items.stem: 다음 요약문의 빈칸 (A), (B)에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은? (곰 겨울잠)
items.explain: 곰의 겨울 휴식은 진짜 동면을 흉낼 뿐(mimics)이고, 체온이 거의 안 떨어져 쉽게 깨울 수 있음(readily arousable).
items.lead: 〈요약〉 A bear's winter rest only (A)＿＿＿ true hibernation; because its temperature barely falls, the bear stays (B)＿＿＿ arousable.

It means when people live without a general power to control them all, they are indeed in a state of war. Moreover, since society is not a natural phenomenon and there is no natural force bringing people together, what will bring them together as a society is not mutual affection according to Hobbes. It is, rather, mutual fear of men's present and future that assembles them, since the cause of fear is a common drive among people in the state of nature.

items.passage: According to Hobbes, man is not a being who can act morally in spite of his instinct to protect his existence in the state of nature. Hence, the only place where morality and moral liberty will begin to find an application begins in a place where a sovereign power, namely the state, emerges. Hobbes thus describes the state of nature as a circumstance in which man's life is "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish and short". It means when people live without a general power to control them all, they are indeed in a state of war. In other words, Hobbes, who accepted that human beings are not social and political beings in the state of nature, believes that without the power human beings in the state of nature are "antisocial and rational based on their selfishness". Moreover, since society is not a natural phenomenon and there is no natural force bringing people together, what will bring them together as a society is not mutual affection according to Hobbes. It is, rather, mutual fear of men's present and future that assembles them, since the cause of fear is a common drive among people in the state of nature.
items.stem: 밑줄 친 it이 가리키는 것으로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 'it alone can end the war'의 it = 주권자(the sovereign). ③war·①freedom은 문맥상 부적절.
items.lead: Hobbes believed people surrender freedom to a sovereign because it alone can end the war of all against all.

items.passage: The big problem with money made by governments is that those who run the government are always faced with the temptation to make more of it and spend it. Whether among ancient kings or modern politicians, this has happened again and again over the centuries, leading to inflation and the many economic and social problems that follow from inflation. For this reason, many countries have preferred using gold, silver, or some other material that is inherently limited in supply, as money. It is a way of depriving governments of the power to expand the money supply to inflationary levels. Gold has long been considered ideal for this purpose, since the supply of gold in the world usually cannot be increased rapidly. When paper money is convertible into gold whenever the individual chooses to do so, then the money is said to be "backed up" by gold. This expression is misleading only if we imagine that the value of the gold is somehow transferred to the paper money, when in fact the real point is that the gold simply limits the amount of paper money that can be issued.
items.stem: 글의 요지로 가장 적절한 것은? (금본위)
options: Gold is the only real form of money.
The gold standard bought stability at the price of monetary flexibility.
Vaults are the safest place for gold.
items.explain: 금본위는 안정을 주는 대신 통화 유연성을 희생 → ③. ①②④는 지문 밖·극단.

items.passage: The Internet differs from most of the mass media it replaces in an obvious and very important way: it works in two directions. We can send messages through the network as well as receive them, which has made the system all the more useful. The ability to exchange information online, to upload as well as download, has turned the Net into a thoroughfare for business and commerce. With a few clicks, people can search virtual catalogues, place orders, track shipments, and update information in corporate databases. But the Net doesn't just connect us with businesses; it connects us with one another. It's a personal broadcasting medium as well as a commercial one. Millions of people use it to distribute their own digital creations, in the form of blogs, videos, photos, songs, and podcasts, as well as to critique, edit, or otherwise modify the creations of others.
items.stem: 빈칸 (A), (B)에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은? (Unit 크로스 17강 인터넷 × 교과서 L5 우주-지상 통신)
items.explain: 인터넷 = 양방향(two-way) 교환, 우주 임무 = 궤도와 지상의 실시간(real-time) 통신에 의존.
items.lead: 〈요약〉 Just as the Internet made communication a (A)＿＿＿ exchange, Yujin's mission depended on a constant (B)＿＿＿ link between orbit and ground control.

items.passage: 〈요약〉 Both Joseon's uigwe(의궤) and the gold standard show that trust in a system often rests on (A)＿＿＿ records that anyone can (B)＿＿＿.
items.stem: 다음 요약문의 빈칸 (A), (B)에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은? (Unit 크로스 17강 금본위 × 교과서 L4 기록문화)
items.explain: 의궤와 금본위는 모두 누구나 검증(verify)할 수 있는 검증 가능한(verifiable) 기록에 신뢰가 기반한다.

items.passage: On the other hand, stored food may rot over time, may be removed by robbers, or may simply be lost.
items.stem: 밑줄 친 rot의 문맥상 의미와 가장 가까운 것은?
items.explain: rot는 '(음식이) 썩다·상하다'로 decay와 동의. harden(굳다)·freeze(얼다)·ripen(익다)은 무관, multiply는 '증식하다'.

items.passage: Excessive fat accumulations may have a negative effect on an animal's ability to avoid predators.
items.stem: 밑줄 친 Excessive의 반의어로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: excessive(과도한)의 반의는 '부족한' insufficient. abundant·enormous·unlimited·extreme은 모두 '많은·큰' 쪽이라 함정.

items.passage: 밑줄 친 dormancy의 영영 정의로 가장 적절한 것을 고르시오.
items.stem: 다음 영영 풀이(English definition)에 해당하는 것은? (밑줄 친 dormancy)
options: the act of storing food for the winter
a sudden burst of physical energy
a state of greatly reduced bodily activity
the process of converting food into fat
the ability to escape from natural predators
items.explain: dormancy는 신체 활동이 크게 줄어든 휴면 상태(동면). ①식량 저장, ④지방 전환, ⑤포식자 회피는 오답.

items.passage: Stored food is more (A) than body fat because fat contributes to body mass.

items.explain: 문맥상 '효율적·절약되는'의 형용사 economical. economic(③)은 '경제(학)의'로 뜻이 다른 대표 함정. 명사·부사는 형용사 자리에 부적합.

items.passage: Maximum fat deposition increases with body mass (A) maximum food storage is not constrained by body size.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 말로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: '체질량에 따라 증가' vs '크기 제약 없음'의 대조 → whereas. because(이유)·so that(목적)·unless(조건)·as if(가정)은 대조 의미가 없다.

items.passage: Excessive fat may have a negative effect on an animal's ability (A) predators.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 말로 어법상 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: ability + to부정사(형용사적 용법). 'ability to V' 고정 패턴.

items.passage: Many animals must expend energy (A) and protecting their food stores.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 말로 어법상 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: expend energy (in) V-ing 구문이며 뒤의 protecting과 병렬 → 동명사 managing.

items.passage: Many animals pursue a mixed strategy of accumulating both body fat and food, which leads one to ask, "What are the relative advantages and disadvantages of these two forms of energy storage?" Maximum fat deposition increases with body mass whereas maximum food storage is not constrained by body size. This means that animals, especially small animals, can accumulate much greater energy reserves in the form of stored food than they can in the form of body fat. Further, stored food is more economical than body fat because fat contributes to body mass, and metabolic rate increases with body mass. In other words, there is a metabolic expense to maintaining fat. Excessive fat accumulations may also have a negative effect on an animal's ability to avoid predators. And, if maintaining a high body temperature is advantageous, animals might be expected to accumulate more energy in the form of a food store than as body fat. On the other hand, stored food may rot over time, may be removed by robbers, or may simply be lost. Many animals must expend energy managing and protecting their food stores. Eating food and converting it to fat reduces these types of losses and the energetic costs of managing stored food. A large accumulation of body fat adds to an animal's fasting capacity, especially large animals, permitting some animals to enter prolonged dormancy in the relative security of a hibernaculum. Thus, both fat accumulation and food storage have some decided advantages.
items.stem: 다음 빈칸에 들어갈 연결사로 가장 적절한 것은?
items.explain: 식량 저장의 '장점'에서 '단점(상함·도난·분실)'으로 넘어가는 역접 전환 → On the other hand.
items.lead: ...animals might be expected to accumulate more energy in the form of a food store than as body fat. (A), stored food may rot over time, may be removed by robbers, or may simply be lost.

items.passage: According to Einstein's theory, a large mass like the Sun 'bends' space-time. Newton's theory makes no such prediction. This bending of space-time leads to phenomena such as 'gravitational lensing' where the light of distant stars appears to be in different locations when they pass by a large mass like the Sun. We don't normally see this lensing because stars aren't visible during the day when the Sun is out, but a solar eclipse in 1919 allowed scientists to observe what the Sun's gravity was doing to the light from distant stars. The stars around the Sun appeared to have moved from their normal positions in the night sky. The shift was much larger than Newton's theory predicted, but exactly in the positions predicted by Einstein's theory.

items.passage: The Internet differs from most of the mass media it replaces in an obvious and very important way: it works in two directions. We can send messages through the network as well as receive them, which has made the system all the more useful. The ability to exchange information online, to upload as well as download, has turned the Net into a thoroughfare for business and commerce. With a few clicks, people can search virtual catalogues, place orders, track shipments, and update information in corporate databases. But the Net doesn't just connect us with businesses; it connects us with one another. It's a personal broadcasting medium as well as a commercial one. Millions of people use it to distribute their own digital creations, in the form of blogs, videos, photos, songs, and podcasts, as well as to critique, edit, or otherwise modify the creations of others.
items.stem: 글의 흐름상 전체 내용과 관계 없는 문장은? (17강 양방향 인터넷)
items.explain: 글의 주제는 인터넷의 쌍방향·참여성. ⑤'인쇄 신문이 매일 아침 배달'은 흐름과 무관.
items.lead: The two-way internet lets users both receive and create content. ① It allows ordinary people to share their own ideas widely. ② Online platforms now invite participation from almost everyone. ③ This interactivity has turned former audiences into active creators. ④ Communities increasingly form around the content people make. ⑤ Printed newspapers were historically delivered to homes every morning.

items.passage: 〔주어진 문장〕 This is why advertisers so often emphasize limited availability. ── People desire scarce things more strongly. ( ① ) Marketers understand this principle very well and use it constantly. ( ② ) They create a sense of urgency with countdown timers. ( ③ ) Limited editions tend to sell out almost instantly. ( ④ ) Even ordinary items feel special once they seem rare. ( ⑤ )
items.stem: 주어진 문장이 들어갈 가장 적절한 곳은? (13강 희소성)
items.explain: 주어진 문장은 '그래서 광고가 제한성을 강조한다'. 앞 문장 '사람들은 드물면 더 원한다' 바로 뒤 ① 위치가 가장 자연스럽다.

items.passage: Becoming an astronaut begins long before launch day. (A) First, candidates undergo years of rigorous physical and academic training. (B) Then, after final selection, they rehearse every mission step in simulators. (C) Finally, only after countless rehearsals do they actually travel into space.
items.stem: 주어진 글 다음에 이어질 순서로 가장 적절한 것은? (교과서 L5 우주)
items.explain: 시간 순서가 명확: First(A) 훈련 → Then(B) 시뮬레이터 연습 → Finally(C) 비행 → (A)-(B)-(C).

items.passage: the state of being rare and in short supply
items.stem: 다음 영영 정의(English definition)에 해당하는 단어는? (13·15강 공통)
items.explain: '드물고 공급이 부족한 상태' = scarcity. ①surplus(과잉)·②demand(수요)와 혼동 주의.

items.passage: a balance in which gaining one thing means giving up another
items.stem: 다음 영영 정의(English definition)에 해당하는 단어는? (19·17강 공통)
items.explain: '하나를 얻으면 다른 하나를 포기하는 균형' = trade-off. 체지방·금본위의 공통 구조.

items.stem: 전 범위(13·15·17·19강 · 교과서 L4·L5)를 관통하는 가장 포괄적인 주제는? (통합 추론)
options: Tradition is always superior to technology.
Rarity has no real effect on value.
Humans should avoid all forms of risk.
Saving resources is almost always harmful.
Value, progress, and survival all depend on balancing benefits against costs.
items.explain: 희소성·기술·저장·도전을 관통하는 가장 포괄적 명제는 '가치·진보·생존이 편익과 비용의 균형에 달려 있다'. 나머지는 극단적 일반화라 오답.
